goal_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan covers tea houses/banquets, festival foods, senior-center activities, and cultural trips (portable kits, foot care during walking tours, time-zone/snack adjustments). This reflects his slow-paced cultural travel and social rhythm in Suzhou.

goal_alignment.criterion: Family engagement and shared decision-making leveraging wife’s numeracy
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess if roles are assigned (wife supports carb counting, weighing portions, data tracking; granddaughter reminders/emergency practice), with a WeChat family group and simple household routines. This matches his family dynamics and enhances sustainability.

content_alignment.criterion: Regionalized dietary strategy for Jiangsu/Suzhou palate and tea culture
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether diet advice is built around Chinese staples and local dishes (rice, noodles, mantou, Su/Yangzhou cuisine), with concrete lower-GI swaps, cooking methods (steaming, blanching, vinegar use), sugar/oil/sodium cautions for regional classics, festival food strategies, and guidance on tea type, timing, and caffeine effects on glucose—matching his taste and habits.

content_alignment.criterion: Exercise plan alignment with existing habits and time preferences
content_alignment.explanation: Check whether the exercise content leverages his morning/evening routine and current activities (walking, Tai Chi, yoga), provides low-back-friendly variations, scalable intensity suitable for older adults with T2D, and realistic alternatives for weather/air quality—ensuring the chosen activities fit his life rather than introducing unfamiliar sports.

content_alignment.criterion: China-specific health tech and platform fit for glucose monitoring
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate if monitoring solutions reference China-available SMBG/CGM devices, pharmacies, and logistics; integrate with WeChat mini-programs for logging/alerts; support Chinese-language interfaces; and enable family data sharing—so the technology ecosystem matches his tools and local access.

content_alignment.criterion: Family co-management and household context integration
content_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan assigns roles to his wife and granddaughter (e.g., meal planning, after-dinner walks, data review, hypoglycemia support), and includes home-based routines that fit their shared evenings—aligning content with his close-knit family dynamic and increasing adherence.

content_alignment.criterion: Hypoglycemia and emergency protocol localization (China-specific)
content_alignment.explanation: Assess if emergency content reflects local realities: China’s emergency number (120), availability/names of glucagon products, WeChat SOS/location sharing, how to act in parks or community spaces, and instructions suitable for older adults and family—ensuring safety guidance is context-appropriate.

content_alignment.criterion: Evidence depth calibrated to an educated retiree in China
content_alignment.explanation: Check that recommendations cite up-to-date, reputable guidelines (e.g., Chinese Diabetes Society, ADA) and high-quality studies, summarize older-adult glycemic/ BP/lipid targets relevant to his profile, and present key rationale without excessive jargon—matching his education and evidence-seeking preference.

content_alignment.criterion: Travel and eating-out strategies tailored to his domestic trips and dining patterns
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate if the plan covers Chinese train/short-trip packing lists for diabetes supplies, restaurant ordering tips for Su/Yang cuisine, tea tasting considerations, and routines to maintain monitoring/exercise while traveling—reflecting his seasonal cultural tours.

content_alignment.criterion: Data tracking and interpretation aligned to his decision style and family strengths
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the content proposes structured SMBG/CGM patterns (e.g., paired testing around meals he actually eats), simple trend summaries, and collaborative review methods that a math-savvy spouse can help with—fitting his independent yet family-influenced decisions.

content_alignment.criterion: Cost–value tiering consistent with rational spending and risk aversion
content_alignment.explanation: Check if options are presented in tiers (budget SMBG vs. CGM; essential vs. premium apps), with China-relevant price ranges and value trade-offs, avoiding unproven or high-risk interventions—matching his cautious, value-focused mindset.

content_alignment.criterion: Use of local community and environmental resources in Suzhou
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan leverages nearby parks, senior centers, and community classes (e.g., Tai Chi groups), and accounts for local weather/air quality/seasonal patterns—so environment-specific opportunities and constraints shape the proposed content.

presentation_fit.criterion: Phased and modular organization aligned to the task components
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the report is clearly divided into phases (e.g., first 4–12 weeks, maintenance) and modules (diet, exercise, monitoring, emergency) with explicit section headings and a logical progression, so a retired teacher can follow step by step and revisit specific parts easily.

presentation_fit.criterion: Senior-friendly readability and layout
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate sentence length, paragraph density, use of bullet lists, highlight boxes, font size guidance, and contrast-friendly color choices to reduce visual/cognitive load for a 65–70-year-old reading on phone or print.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology and numeracy clarity
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that medical acronyms and metrics (e.g., HbA1c, CGM, mmol/L, mg/dL) are defined on first use, units are consistent, and number ranges/targets are presented with plain-language explanations or mini-glossaries.

presentation_fit.criterion: Emergency protocol visualization (hypoglycemia)
presentation_fit.explanation: Determine whether hypoglycemia handling is presented as an easy-to-scan flowchart or stepwise algorithm with signal words (e.g., ‘立即’, ‘若…则…’) and high-visibility callouts for doses and thresholds to enable rapid action under stress.

presentation_fit.criterion: Use of visual aids for routine behaviors
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess presence and clarity of tables, timelines, and checklists for meal timing/portions, weekly exercise schedules, and glucose monitoring schedules to make daily routines intuitive at a glance.

presentation_fit.criterion: Evidence signposting and citation readability
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether guideline/research sources are cited in a non-intrusive way (e.g., brief source labels, endnotes), with update dates and simple evidence-strength cues, avoiding dense academic formatting.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tone and cultural resonance
presentation_fit.explanation: Judge whether the language is respectful, calm, and non-patronizing, optionally using elegant, plain Mandarin and relatable metaphors without slang or marketing hype, matching a scholarly reader influenced by traditional culture.

presentation_fit.criterion: Navigation aids and concise summaries
presentation_fit.explanation: Check for an executive summary (key targets, daily checklist), table of contents, section breadcrumbs, and end-of-section recaps so the user and family can quickly locate and remember key points.

presentation_fit.criterion: Mobile- and print-friendly formatting
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the layout works well in WeChat-length screens (short sections, jump links) and prints cleanly in black-and-white with preserved hierarchy, plus QR links for resources when appropriate.

presentation_fit.criterion: Relatable scenarios and examples for explanation
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate use of brief, familiar daily-life scenarios (e.g., morning park walk, tea time) to illustrate instructions and thresholds, enhancing comprehension without altering the underlying medical content.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Phased implementation roadmap with concrete weekly actions and milestones
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the report provides a step-by-step timeline (e.g., Week 1 device setup and baseline logging; Weeks 2–4 diet adjustments; Weeks 5–8 exercise progression) aligned to the user’s morning/evening availability, with checklists and milestones so he knows exactly what to do and when.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Diet plan operationalization for the Chinese/Jiangsu context with quantifiable portions
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses if the report translates dietary strategies into executable actions: sample 7-day menus using Suzhou/Yangzhou dishes, portion sizes in grams/liang/household bowls, swaps for rice/noodles, tea guidance (unsweetened), and rules for dining out/food delivery—so he can buy, cook, and order immediately.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Exercise plan feasibility, safety, and progression tailored to current habits
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan integrates walking, Tai Chi, and yoga with clear frequency, intensity (e.g., talk test/RPE), duration, post-meal walking, rainy-day/home alternatives, low-back-friendly options, and a weekly schedule that fits his routine and reduces injury risk.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Blood glucose monitoring protocol with actionable thresholds and pattern-management rules
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for precise SMBG/CGM schedules (timing and frequency), individualized numeric targets for an older adult, and explicit decision rules (e.g., adjust carb portions or add postprandial walking when readings exceed thresholds) so measurements directly inform actions.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Devices, apps, and tools: setup steps, availability in China, and ongoing costs
actionability_practicality.explanation: Looks for specific device/app recommendations (glucometers/CGM), how to set them up, estimated monthly costs for strips/sensors, insurance or reimbursement pointers, and practical tools (kitchen scale, measuring cups) with WeChat-compatible logging/reminders to remove setup barriers.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Hypoglycemia emergency protocol completeness and family readiness
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the report provides the 15–15 rule, carry-on kit checklist (glucose tabs/juice), glucagon availability and training steps for wife/granddaughter, a wallet card, thresholds for calling 120, and WeChat location-sharing instructions, enabling reliable real-world response.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Safety checkpoints and medical clearance for older adults
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies pre-exercise screening guidance, foot care routine, retinopathy/neuropathy precautions, heat/humidity considerations for Suzhou, renal considerations for protein, and flags for medication-related hypoglycemia that prompt clinician consultation—ensuring safe implementation.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Data logging and feedback loop with predefined escalation triggers
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for simple logs/templates, a weekly review routine (with the spouse if helpful), clear rules to modify diet/exercise based on patterns, and clinician escalation triggers (e.g., fasting glucose above a threshold on multiple days; A1c testing cadence) to sustain improvement.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Lifestyle and time integration strategies
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether recommendations fit his morning/evening preference, incorporate movement breaks around calligraphy, coordinate with family meals, and include travel/dining-out contingencies and food delivery ordering rules to maximize adherence in daily life.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Budget feasibility and long-term sustainability
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the plan estimates monthly costs (diet changes, strips/sensors, classes), offers cost-saving substitutions, and prioritizes spend on highest-impact items, supporting sustained adherence for a rational, price-conscious retiree.

depth_insight.criterion: Currency and Critical Synthesis of Evidence and Guidelines
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan integrates up-to-date, high-quality evidence (e.g., ADA/EASD/AACE guidelines, RCTs, meta-analyses), grades the strength of evidence, reconciles conflicting findings, and articulates trade-offs and uncertainties to justify chosen strategies.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization and Risk Stratification
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the depth of tailoring to patient phenotypes and contexts (e.g., obesity, CKD/CVD, hypoglycemia risk, age, medication regimen, cultural diet, schedule, cost/access), demonstrating nuanced clinical judgment beyond one-size-fits-all advice.

depth_insight.criterion: Dietary Intervention Depth and Mechanistic Rationale
depth_insight.explanation: Judges whether dietary recommendations specify evidence-backed patterns and targets (e.g., low-carb vs Mediterranean, carb quality/GL/GI, fiber/protein goals, meal timing), explain mechanisms linking diet to A1c/TIR/weight and complications, and address contraindications/interactions.

depth_insight.criterion: Exercise Prescription Sophistication and Metabolic Insight
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the exercise plan uses a FITT framework with intensity guidance, combines aerobic/resistance/‘exercise snacks,’ times activity relative to meals for postprandial control, and includes evidence-based safety modifications for complications (neuropathy, retinopathy, CKD).

depth_insight.criterion: Glucose Monitoring Strategy and Pattern-Management Algorithms
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the depth of SMBG/CGM use (frequency, timing, TIR/GMI/variability targets), pattern recognition methods, and concrete adjustment rules that translate data into changes in diet, activity, or medications, showing data-driven insight rather than passive tracking.

depth_insight.criterion: Phased, Adaptive Plan with Measurable Milestones and Decision Rules
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan is staged (short-, mid-, long-term) with quantifiable targets (A1c/TIR, weight, BP/lipids), predefined review intervals, and explicit escalation/de-escalation triggers, demonstrating foresight and actionable value.

depth_insight.criterion: Safety and Emergency Protocol Depth (Hypoglycemia and Sick-Day Rules)
depth_insight.explanation: Judges the specificity and evidence basis of protocols (e.g., thresholds for level 1/2 hypoglycemia, grams of glucose, recheck intervals, glucagon use, driving rules, SGLT2i sick-day holds, ketone testing, euglycemic DKA precautions), reflecting high-stakes insight.

depth_insight.criterion: Technology and Tools Appraisal for Long-term Management
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the critical selection and justification of CGM/SMBG devices, apps, and connected tools (accuracy, cost-effectiveness, usability, data sharing/integration, alert quality), matched to patient needs and resources for sustained benefit.

depth_insight.criterion: Behavior Change and Adherence Insights
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the use of evidence-based strategies (e.g., habit formation, motivational interviewing elements, environmental design, social support, addressing sleep/stress) to enhance adherence and sustainability, increasing the real-world value of recommendations.

logical_coherence.criterion: Phased Structural Framework and Progression Criteria
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the plan is organized into coherent phases (e.g., initiation, stabilization, optimization, maintenance) with explicit goals (HbA1c/FBG targets), entry/exit criteria, dependencies (e.g., education before carb counting), and review cadence. A clear phase structure ensures the reasoning unfolds in a controlled, sequenced manner rather than as ad hoc advice.

Evaluates whether each diet, exercise, monitoring, and emergency recommendation is explicitly linked to stated premises (e.g., ADA/EASD guidelines, RCT/meta-analysis findings) and shows how those premises justify specific parameters (e.g., carb targets, MET-min/week, SMBG/CGM frequency). This prevents logical leaps from general evidence to specific prescriptions.

logical_coherence.criterion: Data-Driven Feedback Loops and Adjustment Logic
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether the report specifies if–then rules that translate glucose data (SMBG/CGM) and outcomes into concrete adjustments to diet, activity, or medication coordination, with thresholds, timing, and responsible actions (e.g., review every 2 weeks; if TIR <70%, adjust carb timing; if pre-exercise BG <90 mg/dL with sulfonylurea, add carbs). This closes the loop from measurement to intervention.

logical_coherence.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Domains and Constraints
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks for logical compatibility among sections: diet timing aligns with monitoring schedule; exercise intensity aligns with hypoglycemia risk under current meds; hydration and ketoacidosis warnings align with SGLT2 use; protein targets respect renal status. Consistency avoids conflicting directives and maintains a coherent plan.

logical_coherence.criterion: Explicit Assumptions, Scope, and Applicability Conditions
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether the report states baseline premises (e.g., medication class, comorbidities, renal function, technology access), scope limits, and conditions under which recommendations apply or need modification. Transparent assumptions bound the logical domain and prevent misapplication of the reasoning.

Assesses whether emergency protocols (e.g., hypoglycemia 15/15 rule, sick-day rules, when to seek urgent care) are integrated as stepwise decision pathways with triggers and actions, consistent with the main plan. Clear contingencies maintain logical coherence under deviations and edge cases.

Checks whether transitions explicitly connect outputs of one section to inputs of the next (e.g., dietary carb distribution informs SMBG timing; monitoring insights feed exercise adjustments), avoiding abrupt topic shifts. Smooth connections help readers follow the reasoning chain across sections.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structural Layout and Hierarchy
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether the report uses a clear hierarchy (e.g., phases, sections, subsections), consistent numbering, headings, spacing, and bullet points, making it easy to scan and locate diet, exercise, monitoring, and emergency protocol sections quickly.

clarity_readability.criterion: Procedural Clarity and Actionable Formatting
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether key processes (meal planning steps, exercise routines, glucose monitoring schedules, hypoglycemia actions) are presented as concise, numbered steps or checklists with clear timing/frequency cues, making instructions immediately executable.

clarity_readability.criterion: Language Clarity, Fluency, and Precision
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks that sentences are concise and unambiguous, with consistent tone and precise wording; avoids run-ons and confusing qualifiers so that non-expert readers can follow recommendations without misinterpretation.

clarity_readability.criterion: Use and Explanation of Technical Terms
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether essential terms and abbreviations (e.g., HbA1c, CGM, glycemic index/load, METs, hypoglycemia) are defined at first use or in a glossary, reducing jargon barriers and aiding accurate understanding.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Visuals (Tables, Charts, Flowcharts)
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether visuals are well-labeled and intuitive (e.g., target range tables, weekly exercise schedules, hypoglycemia flowchart), use readable legends and colorblind-safe palettes, and directly support rapid comprehension of key actions and targets.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Critical Safety Information and Targets
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks that thresholds, warnings, and emergency steps are visually emphasized (e.g., callout boxes, icons, bolding) so readers can quickly identify what to do in urgent situations and which targets matter most.

clarity_readability.criterion: Consistency and Standardization of Units, Symbols, and Formats
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses consistent use of units (mg/dL vs mmol/L), time and date formats, and measurement precision; provides conversions where helpful, reducing confusion and calculation errors during implementation.

clarity_readability.criterion: Navigability and Reference Usability
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates the presence of a table of contents, internal cross-references, and clearly formatted links to tools/resources, enabling readers to jump efficiently to sections like emergency protocols or device setup instructions.

task: I am a beginner runner preparing for my first full marathon in the next three months, with the goal of completing the race safely and without injury. I can train about five days a week for roughly one hour per session, mainly running in nearby parks or residential roads. I do not have professional running equipment—only a pair of basic running shoes. I currently have no running experience and no specific pace targets, but I hope to gradually increase my mileage while considering my daily life and work schedule. The training plan should be easy to follow, sustainable, and adaptable. Please create a personalized training plan based on these conditions.

goal_alignment.criterion: Safety-first progressive load for a true beginner
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan prioritizes injury prevention via gradual mileage/load progression (e.g., conservative weekly increases), run–walk starts, warm-up/cool-down, and dorm-friendly mobility/strength focused on hips/glutes/calves/shins, matching Zhou Kai’s zero running base and risk-averse nature.

goal_alignment.criterion: Feasibility reconciliation of 3-month marathon with ~1-hour sessions
goal_alignment.explanation: Check if the plan explicitly acknowledges the difficulty of finishing a marathon from zero in 3 months with mostly 60-minute sessions and proposes realistic strategies (e.g., run–walk finish goal, one gradual longer session or safe weekend doubles, adjusted expectations) without overselling results.

goal_alignment.criterion: Schedule integration with a student’s evening routine
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether sessions are designed for evenings, fit around classes/lab, and offer an easy-to-follow weekly template (5 days, ~60 minutes) with clear placement of key runs/strength that respects 7:00–23:00 routine and occasional weekend late nights.

goal_alignment.criterion: Adaptability via clear decision rules (sleep/exams/weather/soreness)
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine if the plan provides simple if–then rules to swap, shorten, or downgrade sessions when sleep <6–7h, during exam weeks, in rain/heat, or with niggles, preserving consistency while preventing overload—critical for Zhou Kai’s variable academic demands.

goal_alignment.criterion: Cross-training and dorm-friendly strength leveraging existing habits
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether ping-pong is positioned as light recovery (not a hard workout), and whether a minimal-equipment strength plan (15–20 minutes, 2–3x/week) is integrated to reduce common beginner injuries, aligning with his hands-on, pragmatic style.

goal_alignment.criterion: Budget-aware equipment and resource guidance
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for low-cost, high-impact recommendations (e.g., moisture-wicking socks, basic anti-chafe, reflective band for nights), shoe wear checks, and free apps/spreadsheets for tracking, respecting his frugal student budget and basic-shoes constraint.

goal_alignment.criterion: Local environment and night-running safety (Chengdu context)
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan addresses humid/rainy conditions (heat acclimation, hydration/electrolytes, rain options), air/visibility, and safe routes/lighting for parks and residential roads at night, directly matching Zhou Kai’s training setting.

goal_alignment.criterion: Beginner-friendly, mechanically-minded explanations and simple metrics
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether intensity guidance favors talk test/RPE over pace, uses concise, evidence-based rationale that appeals to an engineering mindset (clear logic, minimal jargon), and provides checklists/templates to reduce cognitive load.

goal_alignment.criterion: Nutrition, hydration, and fueling within campus cafeteria budget
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for practical pre/post-run meals, hydration/electrolytes for humidity, GI training for race-day fueling, and low-cost carb options (bananas, steamed buns, rice), considering his preference for spicy foods and student dining reality.

goal_alignment.criterion: Monitoring, milestones, and feedback loops with free tools
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine if the plan includes simple tracking (free app or spreadsheet), weekly reflections, early warning signs (shin pain, persistent fatigue), and milestone checks (e.g., week 4/8 assessments) to adjust load—aligning with his deliberate decision style.

goal_alignment.criterion: Route planning for parks/campus without specialized devices
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether practical guidance is given for measuring distance/time on campus tracks/loops, choosing low-traffic routes, and using a smartphone if available, matching his nearby parks/residential road context and minimal gear.

goal_alignment.criterion: Motivation framing and cultural resonance
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan frames training as a methodical, low-risk project that rewards diligence, offers stress-relief benefits during exams, and optionally points to free Bilibili/Zhihu learning resources—resonating with his pragmatic, frugal values.

content_alignment.criterion: Student time-budget and timetable alignment (5× ~60-min sessions)
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan’s core content is built around short, time-capped sessions that can fit a college schedule (morning/late afternoon), with weekly structure reflecting 5 days of training and respect for his evening study preference—rather than assuming long midweek workouts or flexible daytime availability.

content_alignment.criterion: Novice-safe progression using run–walk and RPE/talk-test guidance
content_alignment.explanation: Check that the content chooses conservative mileage ramping, run–walk starts, and effort guidance by RPE/talk test (no pace requirements), matching a zero-experience runner who prioritizes finishing safely and is risk-averse to injury.

content_alignment.criterion: Chengdu-specific environment and safety considerations
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the content accounts for local humidity, frequent rain, and air quality (PM2.5) with practical route/surface choices (parks/residential roads), night/traffic safety on/near campus, and weather/air quality strategies relevant to Chengdu.

content_alignment.criterion: Resource- and budget-conscious gear and fueling choices
content_alignment.explanation: Ensure the content aligns with his frugality and basic shoes by prioritizing minimal gear (e.g., socks/blister care, simple reflective gear), low-cost recovery, and affordable fueling/hydration solutions available via dorm/cafeteria, avoiding expensive gadgets.

content_alignment.criterion: Engineering-oriented feedback loops and simple tracking metrics
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan includes straightforward, evidence-informed metrics (weekly minutes/km, RPE logs, conservative ramp rules, simple injury red flags) that satisfy his analytical mindset without overwhelming jargon or requiring costly devices (use phone apps).

content_alignment.criterion: Adaptability with clear conditional branches for common constraints
content_alignment.explanation: Check for content that explicitly addresses if-then adjustments for exam weeks, rain, poor air quality, or fatigue (e.g., swap days, cut volume, deload weeks), reflecting his variable student schedule and risk-averse profile.

content_alignment.criterion: Technique, warm-up/cool-down, and injury prevention content suited to beginners
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate inclusion of beginner-appropriate cues (cadence/form basics), simple warm-up/cool-down routines, and lower-limb strength/mobility emphasis to match his basic shoes and no-experience status, supporting his primary safety goal.

content_alignment.criterion: Campus-resource-based cross-training and strength integration
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether content leverages available campus options (ping-pong as light cross-training, dorm-friendly bodyweight strength) and blends them into the plan’s structure in a way that respects his current exercise habits.

content_alignment.criterion: Cultural and dietary alignment (Sichuan cuisine, cafeteria, baking interest)
content_alignment.explanation: Check that fueling and GI guidance consider spicy food timing, affordable cafeteria options (e.g., rice, congee, eggs, bananas), and simple homemade carbs aligned with his baking interest, making nutrition advice realistic and personally resonant.

presentation_fit.criterion: Modular week-by-week layout with consistent substructure
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that the plan is organized by week with repeatable sections (e.g., Goal, Sessions, Rest, Notes) and bullet lists so a beginner can quickly find what to do without reading dense paragraphs.

presentation_fit.criterion: Beginner-friendly terminology and intensity representation
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether technical running terms (e.g., LSD, cadence, VO2max) are minimized or briefly defined on first use, and whether intensity is communicated via simple cues (RPE scale, ‘conversational pace’) instead of advanced metrics.

presentation_fit.criterion: Clarity, brevity, and supportive tone
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate if the language is plain (preferably in Mandarin for this user), concise, and encouraging without hype, matching a risk-averse beginner aiming for safe completion.

presentation_fit.criterion: Mobile-friendly skimmability (headings, bullets, TL;DR)
presentation_fit.explanation: Determine whether the report uses clear headings, numbered steps, and short bullet points with an upfront summary/TL;DR so the user can skim on a phone during busy schedules.

presentation_fit.criterion: Safety and adaptation callouts visually emphasized
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that safety notes, red flags, and ‘if-then’ adjustments (e.g., if sick, if time-crunched) are set off with clear labels (Note/Warning/Adjust) so they stand out.

presentation_fit.criterion: Localized units and conventions
presentation_fit.explanation: Verify use of km, minutes, Celsius, 24-hour time, and a Monday-start weekly view to reduce cognitive load for a student in Chengdu.

presentation_fit.criterion: Clear labeling of essentials vs optional items
presentation_fit.explanation: Ensure any gear or add-on suggestions are visually separated and labeled as ‘optional’ to respect the user’s frugal, cost–benefit mindset without implying necessity.

presentation_fit.criterion: Simple progress-tracking templates
presentation_fit.explanation: Look for compact log/checklist formats (e.g., Date–Duration–RPE–Notes) that can be screenshotted and reused, aiding adherence through presentation rather than content.

presentation_fit.criterion: Light, engineering-friendly justifications in side-notes
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether brief ‘why this works’ snippets or rules-of-thumb are presented as small callouts, satisfying curiosity without derailing readability.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Progressive overload with explicit safety safeguards
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the report specifies week-by-week volume and long-run progression (e.g., run–walk structure, ≤10–15% weekly increase, planned deload weeks), caps for long-run jumps, and pain-based stop rules to prevent injury within a compressed 12-week novice timeline.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Time-budget adherence and executable scheduling
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates if most sessions fit the stated 60-minute, 5-days/week limit and evening availability. For essential longer long runs, the plan should either schedule them sparingly with clear justification or offer acceptable workarounds (e.g., split run with trade-offs), plus a sample weekly timetable around classes/lab and ping-pong.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Adaptability and decision rules for real-life disruptions
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the plan includes actionable A/B options and decision trees for exam weeks, illness, missed sessions, bad weather, or poor AQI (what to skip, what to swap, how to resume without overloading).

actionability_practicality.criterion: Injury prevention and recovery routines with minimal equipment
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for concrete warm-up/cool-down protocols and a 2–3x/week bodyweight strength/mobility plan doable in a dorm, plus rest/sleep guidance and simple soreness/pain rules, supporting sustainable training without extra cost.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Race-day readiness, taper, and go/no-go safety gate
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies that the report provides a taper plan, a conservative race-day pacing/run–walk strategy, and objective readiness checkpoints (e.g., ability to complete specific long-run milestones) with a safe option to defer or switch distances if unmet.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Practical nutrition and hydration guidance for a student budget
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan gives cafeteria-friendly pre/during/post fueling (cheap carbs, timing), GI-safe advice considering spicy foods, hydration/electrolytes with low-cost options (e.g., homemade), and practice during long runs.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Environmental and route safety adjustments for Chengdu parks/roads
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for actionable thresholds and alternatives for AQI, heat/humidity, rain, and traffic (e.g., move indoors, reschedule to evening, shorten/replace), and simple route measurement (loops, campus paths) to train safely.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Budget-constrained gear and fueling practicality
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the plan works with basic shoes and recommends only cost-effective essentials that materially reduce risk (e.g., anti-chafe, socks, reflective band/headlamp alternatives), plus low-cost fueling substitutes instead of expensive gels.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Low-tech monitoring and feedback mechanisms
actionability_practicality.explanation: Determines if the plan uses RPE/talk test zones, simple logs, and milestone checks instead of pace/advanced devices, enabling the user to regulate effort and adapt progression without buying gadgets.

depth_insight.criterion: Constraint-aware personalization and feasibility alignment
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan deeply tailors recommendations to the runner’s exact constraints (beginner status, 3-month horizon, 5 days/week, ~60-minute sessions, parks/roads, basic shoes) and provides a realistic, safety-first completion strategy (e.g., run-walk approach, conservative mileage caps) rather than a generic plan.

depth_insight.criterion: Evidence-based progression and periodization under time limits
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the rigor and specificity of the training progression (base-build-deload-taper), long-run strategy within 60-minute sessions (e.g., back-to-back runs, run-walk ratios), and use of proven methods (strides, hills, cadence drills) to safely build endurance and resilience.

depth_insight.criterion: Injury-risk mitigation with monitoring and modification thresholds
depth_insight.explanation: Examines whether the plan proactively limits risk via load management (volume/intensity caps, weekly increases), integrated strength/mobility, recovery tactics, and explicit decision thresholds (e.g., pain duration/location, RPE/HR cues) that trigger rest, regressions, or medical referral.

depth_insight.criterion: Adaptive decision rules for life/work and contingencies
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the presence of clear, practical rules to adjust training for missed sessions, fatigue, variable availability, travel, or adverse weather, ensuring sustainability without undermining the overall training stimulus.

depth_insight.criterion: Fueling, hydration, pacing, and race rehearsal insights within constraints
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan provides actionable guidance on marathon fueling/hydration and pacing (e.g., RPE/talk test, run-walk timings) and shows how to practice these within mostly 60-minute sessions (e.g., targeted weekend sessions, mock segments).

depth_insight.criterion: Environment and equipment-specific operational insights
depth_insight.explanation: Checks for context-aware safety and comfort guidance based on parks/roads and basic shoes (e.g., surface selection, traffic/lighting, blister prevention, lacing, terrain management), adding practical depth beyond standard advice.

depth_insight.criterion: Originality and added value beyond common templates
depth_insight.explanation: Rewards creative, evidence-informed solutions that materially improve safety and feasibility (e.g., conservative weekly ceilings, back-to-back endurance stimuli, micro-dosed strength within runs, smart taper for novices) rather than rehashing generic beginner plans.

logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the report organizes content from goals and constraints to training principles, then to macrocycle (3 months), mesocycles (build/cutback blocks), and weekly microcycles. A hierarchical framework ensures recommendations are systematically derived rather than assembled ad hoc.

Assesses whether all plan elements respect stated constraints (5 days/week, ~60 min per session, parks/roads, basic shoes, beginner status, safety-first). Any deviation must be explicitly justified. Violations indicate logical inconsistency between premises and prescriptions.

Evaluates whether each session type and weekly layout is explicitly linked to underlying premises or training principles (e.g., easy runs for aerobic base, long run for endurance, rest for recovery), allowing a reader to trace every recommendation back to a reason.

Examines whether volume/intensity progress logically (e.g., gradual long-run increases, cutback weeks, rest days) with explicit rules that prevent jumps. Coherent progression is essential to support the injury-prevention goal.

Checks for clear if/then rules (e.g., responses to pain, unusual fatigue, missed sessions, adverse weather) that align with the overall plan logic and constraints, ensuring consistent decisions rather than ad hoc changes.
Assesses whether sections connect logically (e.g., constraints informing principles, principles informing schedules) and whether the report signals dependencies to avoid logical jumps between parts.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structural Layout and Navigability
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report uses a clear heading hierarchy and section flow (e.g., Overview, Safety Notes, Warm-up/Cool-down, Weekly Plan, Adaptation Rules, Taper, Race Day Checklist) with a table of contents or section anchors, so beginners can quickly find what they need.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity of Schedule/Calendar Presentation
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether the week-by-week and day-by-day plan is presented in clean tables or calendars with consistent units (time, distance), explicit session durations (~60 minutes), clear labels (easy, long run, rest, cross-training), and legible highlights for rest days, making the plan easy to follow at a glance.

clarity_readability.criterion: Plain, Precise, and Concise Language
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether instructions use simple, unambiguous phrasing, active voice, and concise steps; includes quantified cues (e.g., RPE ranges, minutes, reps) to minimize guesswork, and avoids long, complex sentences that hinder comprehension.

clarity_readability.criterion: Definition and Consistent Use of Technical Terms
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks that beginner-relevant terms (e.g., RPE, easy pace, long run, strides, cadence, taper, dynamic vs. static stretching) are explained at first use or in a brief glossary and used consistently thereafter to reduce confusion.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Visual Aids (Tables, Charts, Diagrams)
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether visuals (weekly calendars, mileage progression charts, warm-up flow diagrams, checklists) are clean, labeled with legends, readable on mobile/print, and directly reinforce the text without clutter or ambiguity.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Safety and Constraint Cues
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether critical notes (injury red flags, stop criteria, hydration reminders, surface cautions, shoe wear cues) are visually emphasized (callout boxes, icons, bold) and easy to locate, improving safe execution.

clarity_readability.criterion: Scannability of Adaptation Instructions
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether adjustment rules for busy days or missed sessions are formatted as clear, scannable if-then bullets/decision boxes with simple steps (e.g., swap days, reduce volume by X%), enabling quick application without re-reading the whole report.

task: I am a beginner runner preparing for my first full marathon in the next three months, with the goal of completing the race safely and without injury. I can train about five days a week for roughly one hour per session, mainly running in nearby parks or residential roads. I do not have professional running equipment—only a pair of basic running shoes. I currently have no running experience and no specific pace targets, but I hope to gradually increase my mileage while considering my daily life and work schedule. The training plan should be easy to follow, sustainable, and adaptable. Please create a personalized training plan based on these conditions.

goal_alignment.criterion: Safety-first progressive load for a near-novice within a 3-month horizon
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan explicitly acknowledges the compressed timeline and beginner status (resolving any experience ambiguity via a baseline walk/run test), uses conservative progression (e.g., run–walk, capped weekly increases, recovery weeks), includes clear red flags/stop rules, and prioritizes finishing uninjured over mileage targets—matching her risk-averse nature and primary goal.

goal_alignment.criterion: Schedule fit and adaptability to a law student’s week and 60-min sessions
goal_alignment.explanation: Check if the weekly microcycle leverages her morning clarity, respects debate/team activities and study peaks, and fits ~60-minute sessions across 5 days. Look for explicit contingencies (exam weeks, missed runs, travel), and viable strategies for long-run development within time limits (e.g., occasional negotiated 75–90 min, or back-to-back 60-min runs), ensuring sustainability with her dorm/study routine.

goal_alignment.criterion: Novice-friendly intensity guidance without pace targets
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether intensity is prescribed via RPE/talk test (and optionally simple HR zones) rather than pace, includes simple field assessments to set starting effort, and provides clear cues to avoid overreaching—aligning with her lack of pace goals, analytical style, and need for safe execution.

goal_alignment.criterion: Integrated strength, mobility, and warm-up/cool-down prehab
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine if the plan builds in 2x/week short strength sessions (hips/glutes/core, calves), dynamic warm-ups, and cooldown mobility addressing common student/female runner risks and her neck/shoulder tightness—packaged to fit within or adjacent to 60-minute windows and her yoga habit, directly reducing injury risk.

goal_alignment.criterion: Budget- and equipment-conscious guidance
goal_alignment.explanation: Verify that recommendations work with basic shoes and limited funds, prioritizing only essential, cost-effective items (e.g., sports bra, moisture-wicking socks, simple reflective band), phone-based tracking, and low-cost recovery tools or DIY alternatives—respecting her frugal, practical consumption style.

goal_alignment.criterion: Beijing-specific environment and female safety considerations
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan accounts for local AQI (run timing or indoor alternatives on poor-air days), traffic and route safety in parks/residential roads, seasonal heat/cold strategies, hydration access, lighting, and personal safety practices for a young woman running alone—ensuring context-aware, safe training.

goal_alignment.criterion: Student-friendly nutrition, hydration, and fueling strategy
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for practical, low-cost fueling/hydration plans (cafeteria-based options, simple carb/sodium sources), progressive GI training on long runs, day-to-day protein and iron-awareness for a female endurance athlete, and timing around classes—matching her budget, dorm life, and health focus.

goal_alignment.criterion: Clear adaptation and decision rules for disruptions and niggles
goal_alignment.explanation: Look for explicit if-then rules to modify or skip sessions after missed days, early injury signs, poor sleep, or AQI spikes, plus cross-training swaps (e.g., gym bike) and periodic checkpoints to adjust volume—crucial for sustaining progress within her dynamic academic schedule.

goal_alignment.criterion: Structured, evidence-aware communication and tracking support
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan is logically organized (checklists, weekly templates), briefly cites rationale or best practices, integrates with a simple app or spreadsheet for metrics and a weekly reflection (suited to her analytical, self-disciplined style and evening review habit), enhancing adherence.

content_alignment.criterion: Novice-safe load progression within 12 weeks and ~60-minute sessions
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan selects conservative, beginner-appropriate progressions (e.g., run–walk structure, time-on-feet focus, gradual weekly increases with cutback weeks) that respect a ~1-hour cap and transparently handle the long-run constraint. This directly aligns with her zero-experience status, safety-first goal, and fixed session duration.

content_alignment.criterion: Schedule fit to student routine (morning preference, 5 days/week, weekend debate)
content_alignment.explanation: Check if content positions key runs in mornings, distributes 5 sessions of ~60 minutes to avoid clashes with heavy study blocks and debate team activities, and sequences easy/quality/cross-training days to match her weekly rhythm. This aligns the plan with her actual life pattern, improving feasibility without changing style.

content_alignment.criterion: Beijing-specific environment and route safety customization
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan includes AQI/weather adjustments (e.g., thresholds and indoor alternatives), surface/route guidance for parks and residential roads, traffic and personal safety notes, and seasonal layering for Beijing. Local context tailoring ensures the content is usable in her real environment.

content_alignment.criterion: Low-budget equipment and surface guidance aligned to basic shoes
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether gear content prioritizes essential, cost-effective items (e.g., socks, sports bra, reflective aid), lacing/foot-care tips, and surface choices that mitigate impact given basic footwear, without upselling nonessential tech. This matches her student budget and current gear status.

content_alignment.criterion: Beginner-friendly metrics and rationale (time/RPE/talk test, minimal jargon)
content_alignment.explanation: Check that intensity and progression are framed with time-based prescriptions, RPE/talk test, and simple cues instead of pace targets, with concise evidence-based reasons for each session type. This fits her no-pace-target starting point and analytical mindset without overcomplication.

content_alignment.criterion: Targeted injury prevention for her profile (neck/shoulder, female-specific)
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate inclusion of brief, specific strength/mobility selections for hips/glutes/calves and neck/shoulder care, plus female-specific considerations (e.g., cycle-aware load notes). This directly reflects her health history and risk-averse preference, shaping what preventive content is included.

content_alignment.criterion: Lifestyle adaptability rules (exams, travel, debate, dog-walking)
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan contains explicit swap/skip logic, minimum effective sessions, and active-recovery options (e.g., integrating dog walks), tied to exam weeks and events. Adaptation rules tailored to her routine increase alignment without evaluating presentation style.

content_alignment.criterion: Use of simple digital tools and reflection aligned to her habits
content_alignment.explanation: Check for suggestions to leverage free app features (logging RPE/time, streaks), weekly check-ins, and brief reflections that align with her evening summary habit and psychology interest. This matches her digital behavior and supports sustained adherence through content choices.

presentation_fit.criterion: Clear hierarchical structure with an at-a-glance summary and week-by-week organization
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks for a top summary (goals, weekly pattern) followed by clearly labeled sections (Week 1–12), headings, and numbered steps. A legal-student reader benefits from an outline-like hierarchy to quickly locate information and understand progression without rereading.

presentation_fit.criterion: Beginner-friendly terminology and unit localization (with micro-definitions)
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether any running jargon (e.g., RPE, cadence, tempo) is minimized or briefly defined in one line when first used, and whether units are consistent and localized (km/minutes). This aligns with a novice runner in China and reduces cognitive friction.

presentation_fit.criterion: Mobile-friendly scannability (short paragraphs, bullet lists, concise line lengths)
presentation_fit.explanation: Assesses if content is chunked into short, skimmable blocks with bullets and minimal wall-of-text, suitable for reading on a phone and for quick daily consultation between classes or commutes.

presentation_fit.criterion: Explicit weekly schedule visualization with day labels, time-of-day tags, and estimated durations
presentation_fit.explanation: Looks for a consistent day-by-day layout (e.g., Mon–Sun), optional morning/evening tags, and visible duration estimates per session. This helps a time-structured student map sessions into her routine and quickly see when to run.

presentation_fit.criterion: Safety and recovery items visually emphasized (callouts/checklist-style blocks)
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks whether warm-up/cool-down, rest days, and injury-warning notes are separated or highlighted via callouts or checklist-style bullets. A risk-averse beginner needs these cues to stand out from the main text.

presentation_fit.criterion: Adaptation options presented as clear if-then blocks or labeled variants
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether adjustments (e.g., exam weeks, bad weather, time squeeze) are presented in a structured, easily identifiable format (if-then/option A-B) rather than buried in paragraphs, supporting quick decision-making under changing schedules.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tone fit: supportive, professional, and non-competitive
presentation_fit.explanation: Assesses whether the language is encouraging and calm, avoids macho/elite-runner bravado, and maintains an evidence-respecting tone suitable for a logical law student starting from zero experience.

presentation_fit.criterion: Consistency of labels, notation, and formatting across the entire report
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks for consistent use of day names, abbreviations, units, numbering, and section headers week-to-week. Consistency reduces confusion and aligns with the user’s preference for orderly, reliable presentation.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Safe, progressive load structure tailored to a true beginner (run–walk, cutbacks, long-run progression)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the plan builds volume conservatively with clear week-by-week mileage/time guidance, uses run–walk and cutback weeks, and relies on RPE/talk test instead of pace—so a novice can progress without injury.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Schedule and logistics fit for 5× ~60-minute sessions within a student routine
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether sessions reliably fit into 60-minute morning/evening windows with minimal setup/commute, account for debate/mock court on weekends, and suggest micro-adjustments to protect study time.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Explicit resolution of long-run feasibility under the 60-minute constraint
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report directly addresses the need for longer endurance work (e.g., scheduled occasional extended weekend long runs, split runs, or run–walk strategies), clarifies trade-offs, and provides a realistic path to safe marathon completion.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Injury prevention and recovery protocols integrated into the weekly plan
actionability_practicality.explanation: Looks for warm-up/cool-down steps, beginner-friendly strength/prehab (hips/glutes/core) in 10–20 minutes, neck/shoulder care, sleep and recovery guidance, rest days, and pain rules that keep her uninjured.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Adaptability decision rules for common disruptions (AQI/weather, illness, menstruation, exams, travel)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for simple if–then rules and swap options (indoor cross-training, rescheduling, volume/intensity reductions) to keep training sustainable when conditions change.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Operational clarity of each workout
actionability_practicality.explanation: Ensures every session has concrete steps (minutes, run–walk ratios, RPE targets, warm-up/cool-down, strength exercise names/sets/reps) so she can execute without guesswork or extra research.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Minimal-equipment practicality and budget guidance
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies that the plan works with basic shoes and suggests only prioritized, low-cost safety essentials (e.g., reflective band, headlamp, sports bra), using a phone/fitness app instead of specialized gear.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Training and race-day fueling/hydration that is simple and budget-friendly
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the report provides easy pre/during/post-run nutrition, hydration/electrolyte guidance, GI practice timelines, and campus-cafeteria-compatible options to support safe completion.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Route and personal safety logistics for parks/residential roads in Beijing
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for actionable guidance on safe times/lighting, loop planning near campus, traffic awareness, AQI thresholds, and personal safety tips relevant to a young female runner.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Monitoring checkpoints and adjustment triggers
actionability_practicality.explanation: Looks for simple periodic tests (e.g., 5 km or 30-min talk test), soreness/fatigue scales, and clear thresholds for stepping back or seeking medical advice to manage risk and maintain progress.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Race-day execution plan (run–walk pacing, logistics, contingency)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report provides a start-to-finish action plan: arrival, warm-up, early pacing, fueling intervals, walk breaks, heat/cold/AQI contingencies, and finish-line safety steps.

depth_insight.criterion: Feasibility and Risk Trade-off Appraisal for a Novice with 12 Weeks and 1-hour Sessions
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan explicitly evaluates the realism of finishing a marathon under these constraints, frames expectations, and proposes safety-first contingencies (e.g., run-walk completion, reconsidering race timing if red flags appear). This shows mature judgment rather than blindly prescribing mileage.

depth_insight.criterion: Evidence-based Periodization and Load Progression Within the Time Cap
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan applies training science (base/build/taper, progressive overload with cutback weeks, safe weekly increments, intensity distribution) tailored to five one-hour sessions, demonstrating mechanism-level reasoning rather than a generic schedule.

depth_insight.criterion: Endurance Development Strategy Without Traditional Long Runs
depth_insight.explanation: Checks for creative, justified methods to build marathon endurance under a 60-minute ceiling (e.g., back-to-back easy runs, structured run-walk, time-on-feet emphasis, brisk walking/hiking, fueling practice within shorter sessions), revealing insight in constraint-driven design.

depth_insight.criterion: Injury Prevention Strategy Tailored to Minimal Gear and Park/Road Surfaces
depth_insight.explanation: Looks for targeted, evidence-aligned safeguards for a beginner with basic shoes on hard surfaces: micro-dose strength/mobility, cadence/form cues, surface/route selection, shoe management, and explicit pain thresholds—indicating depth in risk mitigation.

depth_insight.criterion: Adaptive Monitoring and Decision Rules
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan specifies how to monitor effort and fatigue (RPE, talk test, simple HR guidance, soreness scales) and provides clear if-then adjustments for missed sessions, illness, or excessive fatigue, demonstrating rigorous, responsive planning.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization to Schedule and Environment
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates how well the plan leverages five weekly one-hour blocks, accommodates work/life variability, and utilizes parks/residential roads (e.g., loop logistics, water access, traffic safety), showing practical tailoring for sustainability.

depth_insight.criterion: Race-day Execution and Safety Plan Aligned with Training Approach
depth_insight.explanation: Checks for a coherent finish-oriented race strategy derived from training (conservative pacing or run-walk ratios, hydration/fueling plan, heat/cold contingencies, cutoff awareness), adding actionable value beyond training weeks.

depth_insight.criterion: Original, Actionable, Context-specific Insights
depth_insight.explanation: Rewards unique, high-value tips that fit the constraints (e.g., park-loop fueling stashes, blister prevention with basic gear, micro-strength within warm-up/cool-down), distinguishing deep expertise from boilerplate advice.

logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether user goals (finish safely, injury-free) and constraints (novice, 5 days/week, ~1 hour sessions, parks/roads, basic shoes, 3-month horizon) are explicitly mapped to a phased plan (e.g., base, build, peak/taper) with clear purposes and traceable decisions for each phase.

logical_coherence.criterion: Progressive Overload and Recovery Logic Across 12 Weeks
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether weekly volume, long-run distance, and workout difficulty progress in a controlled manner (e.g., gradual increases, cutback weeks, taper), with rest/recovery placement that logically supports adaptation and reduces injury risk for a beginner.

Assesses internal consistency between plan elements and stated constraints: session durations near 1 hour (with justified exceptions for long runs), five training days allocated rationally, surface/equipment constraints reflected in workout types, and numeric checks (e.g., weekly increase rates, distribution of stress) free of contradictions.

logical_coherence.criterion: Justification Linkage Between Choices and Training Principles
logical_coherence.explanation: Examines whether key prescriptions (e.g., easy-pace emphasis, cross-training vs. speedwork, taper timing) are explicitly linked to relevant training principles or factual reasoning, avoiding arbitrary rules and ensuring conclusions follow from stated premises.

logical_coherence.criterion: Coherent Transitions Between Sections and Schedule Components
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks that the report uses logical bridges from goals/constraints to strategy, from strategy to weekly templates, and from templates to progression and taper, preventing jumps or disjointed shifts that would break the reasoning flow.

Evaluates whether clear if-then rules guide adjustments for schedule conflicts, fatigue, minor pain, or weather, and whether these rules preserve the plan’s core logic (e.g., maintaining recovery after long runs, keeping progression intact after missed sessions).

clarity_readability.criterion: Structured Weekly Schedule and Day-by-Day Layout Clarity
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether weeks are clearly labeled and easy to navigate, with each day’s session presented in a consistent pattern (e.g., Warm-up / Main / Cool-down, duration noted), enabling quick ‘what to do today’ scanning.

clarity_readability.criterion: Language Simplicity and Instructional Precision
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether instructions use plain, concise language (short sentences, active voice), avoiding ambiguity (e.g., exact durations and steps) so a beginner can follow without prior knowledge.

clarity_readability.criterion: Technical Term and Intensity Scale Explanation with Consistent Units
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether all jargon and abbreviations (e.g., RPE, tempo, strides) have clear definitions or a legend, and whether units (minutes vs km, pace vs effort) are used consistently to prevent confusion.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Visual Aids (Tables, Calendars, Checklists)
clarity_readability.explanation: Examines whether week-by-week tables/calendars, checklists, or simple charts are used to summarize sessions, track mileage/time, and present progress clearly, reducing cognitive load.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Actions, Rest/Safety Notices, and Modifications
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether critical information (rest days, injury warnings, surface/shoe notes, hydration reminders) and simple modifications are visually emphasized (e.g., callouts, icons, bold) for quick recognition.

clarity_readability.criterion: Adaptability and Variant Labeling Readability
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether optional variants and time-boxed alternatives are clearly labeled (e.g., ‘Optional’, ‘If short on time, do X’), using simple if-then phrasing to help readers adjust sessions without guesswork.

clarity_readability.criterion: Formatting Cleanliness and Consistency
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates consistent use of headings, numbering, bullet points, spacing, and typography so sections are visually organized and scannable across the entire document.

task: I am a beginner runner preparing for my first full marathon in the next three months, with the goal of completing the race safely and without injury. I can train about five days a week for roughly one hour per session, mainly running in nearby parks or residential roads. I do not have professional running equipment—only a pair of basic running shoes. I currently have no running experience and no specific pace targets, but I hope to gradually increase my mileage while considering my daily life and work schedule. The training plan should be easy to follow, sustainable, and adaptable. Please create a personalized training plan based on these conditions.

goal_alignment.criterion: Safety-first, realistic goal framing with risk gating for a near-beginner in 12 weeks
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan explicitly prioritizes injury prevention and safe completion by proposing conservative strategies (e.g., run-walk approach, cutback weeks), setting evidence-aligned expectations for a first marathon in ~3 months, and including decision gates (e.g., readiness checks, signs to scale back or defer) that match the user’s low risk tolerance and analytical style.

goal_alignment.criterion: Progression and microcycle design that honor 60-minute sessions and 5-day availability
goal_alignment.explanation: Check whether weekly structure and mileage growth respect the ~1-hour/session cap and five training days, using methods like gradual volume increases, strategic long-run handling (e.g., capped duration, occasional doubles), and cutback weeks to ensure sustainability within the user’s time limits.

goal_alignment.criterion: Data-guided intensity and adaptation without pace targets
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan uses talk test/RPE or simple HR guidance to define intensities; provides measurable adaptation rules (e.g., how to adjust when RPE/HR drift/fatigue exceed thresholds); and includes a simple tracking/review routine the user can implement with a fitness tracker and Excel, aligning with his data-driven habits.

goal_alignment.criterion: Integrated strength, mobility, and yoga for injury prevention and stress relief
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan leverages his existing yoga practice and weekly gym visit, and prescribes time-efficient prehab (glute/hip/ankle/core) and mobility embedded within or adjacent to runs to reduce injury risk and manage academic stress.

goal_alignment.criterion: Beijing-specific environment and safety guidance
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for tailored advice on AQI-based training adjustments (e.g., treadmill/gym use on high-pollution days), safe route/timing in crowded parks and residential roads, surface selection, seasonal temperature/wind considerations, and urban traffic safety.

goal_alignment.criterion: Budget- and equipment-aware recommendations
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan works with basic shoes and suggests prioritized, low-cost upgrades (e.g., moisture-wicking socks, reflective band/headlamp, simple foam roller), avoiding costly gear, thus fitting the user’s frugal habits while targeting injury prevention and safety.

goal_alignment.criterion: Schedule fit and academic workload adaptation
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan aligns with morning exercise preference, accounts for late bedtime and recovery needs, and provides flexible templates for exam-heavy weeks (e.g., swapping, reducing, or converting sessions), plus ways to integrate occasional hiking as aerobic cross-training.

goal_alignment.criterion: Clear, evidence-informed communication suited to an analytical grad student
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the report is logically structured, concise, and explains the rationale behind each component with authoritative cues; minimizes jargon and offers simple checklists/rules the user can apply independently, matching his decision style and preference for data/facts.

goal_alignment.criterion: Race-day completion strategy and practice
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for a conservative race-day plan (e.g., run-walk ratios, early fueling/hydration, pacing by RPE/talk test), and whether these elements are rehearsed during training within the 60-minute sessions, aligning with the user’s safety goal and lack of pace targets.

content_alignment.criterion: Novice-Safe Progression Without Pace Emphasis
content_alignment.explanation: Checks whether the plan selects beginner-appropriate content (run–walk structure, RPE/talk test guidance, gradual weekly volume increases and cutback weeks) instead of pace-based workouts, aligning with zero-pace-target status and the safety-first aim.

content_alignment.criterion: Alignment to Xu Yang’s Time Budget and Study Rhythm
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether session structures and weekly distributions are chosen to fit five ~60-minute slots, with mornings as light-exercise windows and evenings reserved for reading, and recognize exam/deadline cycles typical of a Beijing graduate student.

content_alignment.criterion: Localization to Beijing Running Environment
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses inclusion of Beijing-specific content: suitable park/road contexts, crowd/traffic timing, air quality (PM2.5/AQI) considerations, seasonal temperature/dryness, and safe lighting—so sessions reflect the real conditions he’ll face.

content_alignment.criterion: Low-Budget, Minimal-Equipment Tailoring
content_alignment.explanation: Checks that content prioritizes cost-effective elements (bodyweight strength/mobility, chafing/blister prevention with low-cost items, sock/insoles tips, reflective hacks) and avoids recommending expensive gear beyond essentials.

content_alignment.criterion: Injury-Prevention and Recovery Content Integrated with Yoga
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan allocates and explains beginner-focused strength/mobility and recovery content and leverages his existing yoga habit to address hips/feet/core, supporting stress relief and reducing injury risk.

content_alignment.criterion: Data-Friendly Tracking and Evidence Orientation
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether content offers simple, analyzable metrics (e.g., weekly mileage, RPE logs, basic HR zones via fitness tracker) and references credible guidelines, matching his analytical, evidence-seeking style (Excel/Python fluency).

content_alignment.criterion: Clear Adaptation Rules for AQI, Weather, and Academic Surges
content_alignment.explanation: Checks for content that specifies when and how to swap, shorten, or replace sessions (e.g., AQI thresholds, indoor alternatives at the gym, travel weeks, sleep-deficit adjustments), reflecting his fluctuating schedule and local conditions.

content_alignment.criterion: Motivation and Preference Fit (Nature, Community, Family)
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether content taps into his interest in nature/hiking (scenic route suggestions, mindful easy runs), optional low-pressure social/accountability elements (Chinese apps/communities), and family/dog-friendly easy run ideas to sustain adherence.

presentation_fit.criterion: Language tone and clarity match a novice runner with an academic background
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the report uses plain, supportive, safety-first language, avoids macho/competitive tone, and keeps rationales concise so a beginner can understand without feeling overwhelmed while still respecting his analytical mindset.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology management and unit consistency
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that training terms (e.g., easy run, RPE, taper, cadence) are introduced with brief, in-line definitions or a legend, acronyms are expanded on first use, and metric units (km/min) are used consistently, reducing cognitive load for a Beijing-based beginner.

presentation_fit.criterion: Structural organization and scannability
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan is phased (Base/Build/Taper), has clear headings/subheadings, bullet lists, and consistent one-hour session templates, enabling at-a-glance understanding and quick insertion into a tight study schedule.

presentation_fit.criterion: Visualization and tabular presentation of the schedule
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess the presence and clarity of weekly calendar tables, simple color/intensity cues, an RPE legend, and a 12-week timeline that are readable on mobile and suitable for screenshot saving and sharing.

presentation_fit.criterion: Clarity of adaptability rules in presentation
presentation_fit.explanation: Judge whether adjustments are presented as simple, scan-friendly if-then rules or decision bullets (e.g., what to do when fatigued or short on time), making adaptability obvious without complex prose.

presentation_fit.criterion: Device- and screenshot-friendly packaging
presentation_fit.explanation: Check for a one-page quick-start summary, checklists, numbered steps, and chunked paragraphs that display cleanly on mobile and are easy to capture as screenshots for later reference.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Progressive overload and injury-risk control
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether weekly mileage and long-run increases are gradual (e.g., ~10% or less), include run–walk options, scheduled rest days, warm-up/cool-down, and basic strength/mobility to prevent overuse injuries in a 12-week novice build.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Operational specificity of the plan
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses if the report provides a clear week-by-week schedule with concrete session goals (durations/distances), simple intensity cues (RPE/talk test/HR caps), long-run progression, cross-training, and taper so the user can execute without ambiguity.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Time-feasibility and schedule integration
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether most sessions fit ~60 minutes on training days and propose practical solutions for necessary longer runs (e.g., weekend window, run–walk pacing, or split-run alternatives) that align with his academic routine.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Adaptation and contingency protocols
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for explicit rules to adjust training for missed sessions, fatigue, minor illness, pain, or heavy workload (e.g., how to downshift a week, when to cap or skip a run, how to resume), minimizing derailment and risk.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Fueling, hydration, and recovery guidance
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies practical pre/during/post fueling for long runs and race day using accessible, budget-friendly options; hydration/sodium guidance; caffeine strategy compatible with his coffee habit; and sleep/recovery tips fitting his midnight–8 a.m. schedule.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Environmental and safety feasibility (Beijing-specific)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses guidance on safe route selection in parks/roads, traffic and low-light safety, air-quality thresholds (AQI-based decisions), cold weather layering, and indoor alternatives (e.g., treadmill at gym) for bad weather/pollution days.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Monitoring and decision gates
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report specifies simple tracking (RPE, talk test, basic HR), weekly progress checks, and readiness criteria (e.g., completion of key long run) to decide when to progress, maintain, or adjust, and whether to toe the start line safely.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Race-day execution practicality
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for a finish-oriented pacing plan (e.g., run–walk ratios), on-course hydration/fuel schedule, and a concise gear/logistics checklist to translate training into a safe, executable marathon day.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Equipment and budget practicality
actionability_practicality.explanation: Ensures the plan works with basic shoes and suggests only low-cost essentials (e.g., reflective band, socks, simple bottle/belt), avoiding expensive gear; includes guidance on when to replace shoes if needed.

depth_insight.criterion: Goal–risk appraisal and alignment of strategy
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan explicitly recognizes the high risk of attempting a marathon from zero in ~12 weeks and adjusts the strategy accordingly (e.g., conservative run-walk emphasis, expectation setting, safety-first milestones), demonstrating mature judgment rather than offering a generic marathon build.

depth_insight.criterion: Integration of user constraints into plan design
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates how well the plan reconciles five ~60-minute sessions, basic shoes, and park/road environments with marathon preparation needs (e.g., time-on-feet solutions, occasional safe extensions or split runs, route/surface choices, footwear care), showing practical, individualized problem-solving.

depth_insight.criterion: Depth of periodization and progressive overload for a novice
depth_insight.explanation: Examines whether the plan applies evidence-informed progression (dominantly easy effort, run-walk progression, weekly volume caps, cutback weeks, and an appropriate taper) grounded in novice physiology and connective tissue adaptation timelines to minimize injury risk.

depth_insight.criterion: Injury prevention and recovery mechanisms
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses inclusion and justification of targeted strength/mobility/prehab, rest spacing, surface/camber guidance, early warning signs, and explicit progression ceilings/stop rules—going beyond generic advice to materially reduce common beginner injuries.

depth_insight.criterion: Intensity prescription without pace or advanced devices
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan provides robust, practical methods to control effort (e.g., RPE/talk test, nasal breathing cues, adjustable run-walk ratios) and explains how to tune them as fitness changes, enabling safe training despite no pace targets.

depth_insight.criterion: Adaptability and decision rules under real-life variability
depth_insight.explanation: Looks for concrete, prioritized adjustment rules for missed sessions, fatigue, illness, travel, or weather (what to drop, what to keep, how to re-enter), including simple monitoring (RPE, soreness, morning feel) that guide week-to-week modifications.

depth_insight.criterion: Fueling and hydration strategy integrated into training
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan introduces and practices fueling/hydration appropriate for progressively longer efforts (e.g., timing, quantities, electrolytes) within equipment limitations (handheld/looping near fountains), to prepare the gut and reduce risk on race day.

depth_insight.criterion: Race-day completion strategy and safety protocols
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the presence of a conservative race execution plan (e.g., run-walk intervals, pacing caps, heat adjustments) and explicit safety criteria for backing off or stopping, translating training insights into a safer marathon-day approach.
