content_alignment.explanation: Assesses if the content includes case-style scenarios/backtests relevant to A-shares and recent macro episodes (e.g., 2015 crash, 2020–2022 swings), matching his preference for case studies and market analysis.

content_alignment.criterion: Personalized Risk–Return Trade-off Framing and Expectation Calibration
content_alignment.explanation: Checks whether the 10% annualized target is contextualized against his risk aversion and six-month horizon, using probability bands or historical ranges to set realistic expectations without hype.

content_alignment.criterion: Cash-Flow–Aware Contribution and Withdrawal Scheduling
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether the content aligns investment pacing with his monthly budget and quarterly family trips (e.g., DCA timing, short-term reserves), reflecting his predictable expense cycle.

presentation_fit.criterion: Professional, neutral, risk-balanced language and tone
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the report uses concise, businesslike language, avoids hype, and explicitly balances return statements with risk caveats—matching an MBA manager’s data-driven and risk-averse mindset.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology precision and localization with brief definitions
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that key financial terms (e.g., annualized return, volatility, drawdown, Sharpe, VaR, ETF) use standard Chinese finance wording with parenthetical acronyms/brief one-line clarifications on first use to reduce cognitive load.

presentation_fit.criterion: Information architecture and scannability
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether content is organized with an executive summary, clearly labeled sections (portfolio, allocation rationale, risk controls, tracking/KPIs), bullet lists, numbered steps, and consistent headings to enable quick skimming in fragmented time.

presentation_fit.criterion: Numerical framing consistency and metric clarity
presentation_fit.explanation: Verify consistent units and timeframes (RMB, annualized vs 6-month equivalents), explicit assumptions, and clear metric definitions so that targets and risk figures are immediately interpretable without ambiguity.

presentation_fit.criterion: Visualization effectiveness and mobile readability
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether charts/tables (allocation pie/bar, risk–return scatter, drawdown curve, scenario table) are few but essential, clearly labeled in Chinese, colorblind-friendly, captioned, and legible on mobile/WeChat screens.

presentation_fit.criterion: Layered depth with TL;DR and navigable structure
presentation_fit.explanation: Check for a top-level TL;DR/key takeaways followed by expandable detail, section numbering, and callouts, enabling quick overview and later deep dive consistent with managerial reading habits.

presentation_fit.criterion: Screenshot-friendly, self-contained panels and tables
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether key summaries/KPI tables are formatted as standalone panels with titles, timestamps, and legends so they can be screenshot and shared on WeChat without losing context.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Implementable portfolio specification and execution roadmap
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report provides concrete target weights, specific instrument types (e.g., domestic ETFs/mutual funds), example tickers/share classes, minimum lot sizes, purchase channels (mainland broker/fund platforms), and a step-by-step, time-bound setup checklist so Liu Qiang can place trades within hours, not days.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Risk suitability mapping and capacity constraints
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report translates Liu Qiang’s risk-averse profile and family obligations into measurable limits (e.g., max portfolio drawdown, per-asset/sector caps, equity allocation ceiling), and ties them to his six-month horizon so the plan is safe to implement.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Return target realism and scenario-based plan
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report assesses whether a ~4.9% six-month target (10% annualized) is realistic for the chosen risk level, provides base/bull/bear scenarios with probability ranges, and specifies actions under each (e.g., de-risking or staying the course).

actionability_practicality.criterion: Maintenance cadence and rules-based rebalancing
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report defines a low-burden monitoring schedule (e.g., monthly), drift thresholds (e.g., rebalance if any sleeve deviates by X%), order-of-trade rules, and acknowledges trading frictions so he can maintain the portfolio with limited time.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Performance tracking framework, benchmarks, and tools
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report specifies KPIs (time-weighted return, volatility, max drawdown), a blended benchmark aligned to the allocation, net-of-fee reporting, and practical tools/apps (domestic broker/fund platforms, spreadsheets) plus alert thresholds to flag underperformance.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Liquidity planning and emergency fund segregation
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report carves out an explicit emergency reserve (e.g., months of expenses) in cash/MMFs, addresses mortgage and upcoming family outlays, and sets rules for deploying or replenishing cash so investments won’t be forced-sold.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Cost, fee, and tax estimation with net-return impact
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report quantifies fund expense ratios, brokerage fees, bid-ask spreads, and outlines applicable taxes/levies in China, then presents expected net returns so decisions reflect actual, implementable outcomes.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Product accessibility and regulatory feasibility (China retail)
actionability_practicality.explanation: All recommended instruments are accessible to a Shanghai retail investor via mainstream platforms, with any required account types (e.g., fund account, securities account) and eligibility steps clearly stated.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Actionable risk controls and contingency procedures
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report provides concrete rules for stress periods (e.g., staged de-risking if drawdown > X% or volatility spike), position limits, and simple hedging alternatives (if any) that do not require complex derivatives or day-to-day trading.

depth_insight.criterion: Goal Translation and Feasibility Assessment
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the 10% annualized target is converted into a six-month return objective, required risk/volatility, and probability of achievement, including trade-offs (expected drawdown, Sharpe needed) and realism given historical ranges.

depth_insight.criterion: Asset Allocation Depth and Diversification Quality
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the portfolio is constructed using expected returns, volatility, correlations, and factor exposures to achieve genuine diversification and balanced risk contributions rather than superficial spread across products.

depth_insight.criterion: Risk Management Framework and Risk Budgeting
depth_insight.explanation: Examines the presence and depth of concrete risk controls (risk budgets, position sizing, rebalancing bands, max drawdown limits, stop-loss/hedging, VaR/CVaR, stress tests) and the cost–benefit reasoning behind them.

depth_insight.criterion: Scenario/Regime Analysis and Adaptive Playbook
depth_insight.explanation: Looks for forward-looking scenario work (e.g., soft landing, recession, inflation re-acceleration), sensitivity to key drivers, and pre-defined adaptive actions with triggers and indicators relevant to a six‑month horizon.

depth_insight.criterion: Instrument Selection and Implementation Insight
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the insightfulness of mapping allocations to specific instruments (ETFs, funds, options, cash) with attention to fees, liquidity, taxes, tracking error, execution tactics (DCA vs lump sum), and operational practicality.

depth_insight.criterion: Performance Tracking, Benchmarking, and Attribution Design
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the report defines suitable benchmarks, measurement methods (time- vs money-weighted), risk-adjusted metrics (Sharpe/Sortino, max drawdown), rolling reviews, and attribution that ties outcomes to allocation and selection.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization and Suitability to Risk Capacity
depth_insight.explanation: Checks whether recommendations reflect the investor’s risk tolerance and capacity (liquidity needs, income stability, emergency fund), with calibrated portfolio variants and guardrails if the 10% target conflicts with risk limits.

depth_insight.criterion: Originality and Actionable Value-Add
depth_insight.explanation: Rewards distinctive, high-value insights (e.g., low-cost hedges, collars, factor/regime tilts, tax-loss harvesting, risk-parity elements) that improve odds of meeting goals within six months beyond standard, generic advice.

logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the report follows a coherent sequence tailored to investment planning: objectives and constraints -> risk profiling -> explicit assumptions -> asset allocation -> product selection -> implementation plan -> risk management -> monitoring/rebalancing -> contingency plans. A clear sequence prevents ad hoc recommendations and anchors every step to prior logic.

Assesses whether each major recommendation (e.g., target allocation, position sizes, rebalancing rules) is explicitly derived from stated premises without gaps or logical leaps. This ensures conclusions are warranted by prior analysis rather than intuition.

Evaluates whether claims (e.g., ‘short duration bonds suit a 6-month horizon’) are explicitly tied to relevant evidence or rationale (risk/return characteristics, liquidity, costs), and that the cited basis is pertinent to the claim. This guards against unsupported assertions while focusing on linkage, not the amount of evidence.

Checks that the 10% annualized target is consistently translated to the 6-month horizon (e.g., annualization method), that risk metrics (volatility, drawdown) and benchmarks match the time frame, and that units are handled consistently. This avoids internal contradictions in targets and measurements.

logical_coherence.criterion: Asset Allocation Logic from Risk Profile and Constraints
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether the proposed asset mix logically follows from the stated risk tolerance, risk capacity, liquidity needs, and 6-month horizon (e.g., equity weight, duration limits, use of cash equivalents). Ensures diversification and risk budgeting are reasoned from the investor’s profile, not chosen arbitrarily.

logical_coherence.criterion: Product Selection Mapping to Target Exposures and Constraints
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks that chosen instruments (e.g., ETFs, funds, bills) unambiguously deliver the intended asset exposures and fit constraints (liquidity, costs, lockups). This prevents exposure mismatches (e.g., illiquid alternatives for a 6-month plan) and maintains logical continuity from allocation to implementation.

logical_coherence.criterion: Risk Management Rules Coherence and Actionability
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether position sizing, stop-loss/drawdown limits, hedging rules, and diversification caps are internally consistent and tied to the stated risk tolerance and targets, with clear thresholds and actions. Coherent rules ensure the risk posture operationally matches the plan’s premises.

logical_coherence.criterion: Monitoring, Rebalancing, and Feedback Loop Logic
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether monitoring metrics, review cadence, rebalancing bands, and trigger-based adjustments logically derive from the strategy and horizon, and whether there is a feedback loop to reassess risk tolerance or assumptions when conditions change. This connects ongoing decisions back to initial logic.

logical_coherence.criterion: Logical Transitions and Cross-Referencing Between Sections
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks that each section explicitly references prior conclusions and sets up the next step (e.g., risk profile informing allocation, allocation informing product picks), avoiding logical jumps between sections. This preserves the continuity of the reasoning chain.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structure, Navigation, and Layout Readability
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether the report has a clear hierarchy (e.g., Executive Summary, Objectives, Risk Profile, Portfolio Recommendation, Risk Management, Tracking & Evaluation, Implementation Checklist), consistent headings, appropriate spacing, and scannable paragraphs/lists so readers can quickly locate the information they need.

clarity_readability.criterion: Language Clarity, Precision, and Accessibility
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether the writing uses plain language, avoids ambiguity and unnecessary jargon, maintains professional tone, and employs concise sentences and active voice so non-experts can easily understand instructions and recommendations.

clarity_readability.criterion: Definition and Consistent Use of Technical Terms and Metrics
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether key terms (e.g., annualized return, volatility, drawdown, Sharpe ratio, beta, VaR, tracking error, rebalancing) are defined at first use, explained in simple terms (and formulas if used), and used consistently to lower the comprehension barrier.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Tables, Charts, and Visual Aids
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether visuals (e.g., allocation tables, pie charts, risk–return plots, benchmark comparison charts, tracking dashboard templates) are well-labeled with titles, units, timeframes, legends, and notes; are uncluttered; use readable fonts/colors; and directly support the text.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Takeaways and Actionable Items
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether crucial information (target allocation percentages, risk limits, rebalancing triggers, benchmarks, checklists) is surfaced via summaries, callouts, bullet points, or bold emphasis to aid scanning and execution.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity of Process, Timeline, and Monitoring Instructions
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether step-by-step procedures for setup, review cadence (e.g., weekly/monthly), performance measurement, drift thresholds, and rebalancing are presented in a clear sequence with timelines and simple tracker templates.

clarity_readability.criterion: Consistency of Units, Timeframes, and Notation
clarity_readability.explanation: Ensures consistent and clearly labeled time horizons (six-month plan vs annualized targets), return expressions (% vs basis points), and risk metrics (monthly vs annualized), preventing misinterpretation during tracking.

clarity_readability.criterion: Transparency of Assumptions and Uncertainty Labeling
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether data sources, historical vs hypothetical examples, scenario ranges, and caveats (no guarantees) are explicitly labeled and separated from facts, reducing the risk of misreading illustrations as promises.

task: I plan to make personal investments over the next six months with the goal of achieving an annualized return of at least 10%. I have no specific preference for the investment field or product type, which could include stocks, mutual funds, or other financial instruments. At the same time, I want to understand asset allocation strategies to better diversify risk and implement effective risk management during the investment process. Please provide me with targeted advice, including a personalized investment portfolio recommendation, as well as methods for tracking and evaluating my investment performance to ensure it aligns with my actual risk tolerance and market changes. I would like your professional guidance to help me plan my investments and achieve my financial objectives.

goal_alignment.criterion: Feasibility and expectation calibration for 10% annualized over a 6‑month horizon
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the report quantifies return–risk tradeoffs and realistically frames the probability of achieving ~10% annualized within 6 months given a conservative profile, including scenario ranges, expected drawdown, and success criteria. This aligns the ambitious target with his rational, data-driven decision style and prevents misaligned expectations.

goal_alignment.criterion: Persona-specific risk capacity and barbell/risk-budget design
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether it defines emergency fund, investable amount, max drawdown tolerance, and a barbell or core–satellite risk budget (conservative core + small high-risk sleeve) reflecting his conservative base, willingness for small high-risk bets, and future home purchase goal.

goal_alignment.criterion: Portfolio construction tailored to index-core preference with a constrained tech tilt
goal_alignment.explanation: Check if the proposed portfolio uses low-cost broad index funds as the core and a carefully sized tech/thematic satellite consistent with his experience, while respecting the 6‑month liquidity/volatility constraints so the design is usable now and consistent with his familiarity.

goal_alignment.criterion: Robust risk management and downside protection
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess inclusion of diversification across asset classes, position sizing rules, portfolio-level drawdown/volatility caps, and simple hedging/substitution (e.g., bonds/gold/defensives) to reduce tail risk in line with his conservative base and desire for effective risk management.

goal_alignment.criterion: Data-driven logic with transparent assumptions and engineer-friendly presentation
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for clear assumptions, parameter choices, and quantitative metrics (expected return, volatility, Sharpe, max drawdown), plus rule descriptions or pseudocode for allocation and rebalancing. This matches his algorithm engineer background and preference for verifiable, model-like guidance.

goal_alignment.criterion: Actionable monitoring and performance evaluation framework with automation hooks
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether it specifies KPIs (TWR/IRR, volatility, max drawdown, Sharpe/Sortino, benchmark comparison), review cadence (weekly/monthly), rebalance triggers, and toolchain suggestions (Python/Jupyter, TuShare/AkShare or spreadsheets) suited to his evening productivity and desire for efficient tracking.

goal_alignment.criterion: Localization to Mainland China products, access, and costs
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether instruments are implementable via Mainland brokers/fund platforms (e.g., A-share ETFs/funds, domestic bonds/money market, QDII/global exposure options), with attention to fees, liquidity, and trading rules to match his cost-effectiveness preference and operating environment.

goal_alignment.criterion: Predefined adaptation to market regime changes
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for simple, robust rules that adjust allocations based on volatility/momentum or other transparent signals, with boundaries and review cadence to avoid ad-hoc reactions, ensuring ongoing alignment with his risk tolerance as markets evolve.

goal_alignment.criterion: Behavioral safeguards against impulsive tech tilts
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate inclusion of pre-commitment checklists, cooling-off periods, position/turnover caps, and a decision journal to curb occasional impulsivity toward tech-heavy bets, tailored to his otherwise rational, independent decision style.

goal_alignment.criterion: Liquidity planning, emergency fund, and DCA integration with salary cash flow
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan reserves an emergency fund, accounts for near-term cash needs (travel, potential pet), uses appropriate cash vehicles, and sets an automated DCA schedule aligned with payday to ensure practical execution without liquidity stress.

content_alignment.criterion: Quantitative, toolable risk/return framework aligned to an algorithm engineer
content_alignment.explanation: Content should emphasize measurable metrics (volatility, max drawdown, Sharpe/Sortino, beta), backtesting logic, and provide tool-friendly avenues (e.g., Python with mainland data sources like Tushare/AkShare, or broker/fund platform exports) so the user can independently verify and iterate, matching his data-driven identity.

content_alignment.criterion: Risk appetite–tailored core–satellite allocation with explicit risk budgets
content_alignment.explanation: Advice should translate his conservative profile with willingness for small high-risk bets into a core of low-risk assets plus a capped satellite (e.g., tech or thematic) with position/risk limits, drawdown caps, or volatility targets—reflecting his stated preferences and tolerance.

content_alignment.criterion: China-specific, implementable product universe and access channels
content_alignment.explanation: Recommendations should use instruments he can actually buy in mainland China (A-share equity/bond index funds/ETFs, money market funds, gold ETFs, onshore REITs, QDII for overseas exposure) and note typical fees/constraints of local brokers/fund platforms, ensuring geographic and regulatory relevance.

content_alignment.criterion: Six-month horizon and liquidity alignment in content selection
content_alignment.explanation: Given his short window and potential future home purchase, content should prioritize liquid, low-lockup products (short-duration bonds, MMFs, liquid ETFs), propose sensible rebalancing/inspection cadence, and frame risk appropriately for a half-year period.

content_alignment.criterion: Monitoring and adaptive rules compatible with his workflow and habits
content_alignment.explanation: Content should include evaluation/tracking methods he can automate and review at night (dashboards, alerts, scheduled reports) and adaptive rules (e.g., volatility targeting, drawdown-based de-risking, regime cues) that map to his request for alignment with risk tolerance and market changes.

content_alignment.criterion: Depth calibration: intermediate-to-advanced, concise, logic-first explanations
content_alignment.explanation: As a technically trained investor with basic-to-intermediate finance knowledge, he needs content that skips beginner definitions and instead offers concise, rigorous explanations (e.g., how risk parity or factor tilts work, simple math or logic), optionally pointing to deeper modules for exploration.

content_alignment.criterion: Cost and implementation efficiency tailored to a cost-conscious profile
content_alignment.explanation: Content should explicitly address expense ratios, tracking error, bid–ask spreads, redemption fees, and relevant taxes/stamp duty where applicable, steering toward low-cost implementations that suit his value-for-money mindset.

content_alignment.criterion: Diversification across assets and factors with tech concentration controls
content_alignment.explanation: Given his interest in tech and index funds, the content should explain correlation structures (China vs global equities, bonds, gold), propose factor diversification (quality/value/low-vol) and set limits/hedges to avoid overconcentration in tech.

presentation_fit.criterion: Structure and scannability tailored to a data-driven engineer (TL;DR, clear hierarchy, numbered steps, tables)
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks whether the report starts with an executive summary, uses clear headings from goals → constraints → portfolio → risk management → tracking, and employs bullet points, numbered procedures, and comparison tables so the user can quickly parse and revisit sections.

presentation_fit.criterion: Precision and consistency of quantitative communication (KPIs, units, sample calculations)
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether investment metrics like annualized return, volatility, drawdown, and Sharpe are precisely defined, units and time frames are consistent (e.g., 6-month horizon vs annualized figures), and sample calculations or mini-examples are shown to prevent misinterpretation.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology density and clarification for a technically literate but non-finance specialist
presentation_fit.explanation: Assesses whether finance terms and acronyms are appropriate for someone with basic investment knowledge and are briefly defined on first use, avoiding jargon overload while maintaining professional rigor.

presentation_fit.criterion: Visualization design fit (clarity, relevancy, and readability of charts and tables)
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks whether visuals (e.g., allocation pie/stacked bar, risk–return scatter, drawdown chart, KPI tables) are clean, annotated, and interpretable with consistent legends and axis labels, avoiding clutter and using readable color schemes suitable for screen viewing.

presentation_fit.criterion: Procedural formatting of tracking and rebalancing plan (checklists, timelines, pseudocode-style steps)
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether performance tracking, alert thresholds, and rebalancing rules are presented as step-by-step checklists, calendar timelines, or algorithm-like instructions that the user can readily operationalize or script.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tone, evidence framing, and uncertainty communication
presentation_fit.explanation: Assesses whether the language is neutral and non-promotional, with clearly boxed assumptions, scenario labels, and uncertainty ranges so the reader understands what is evidence-based vs. assumed without emotional or salesy wording.

presentation_fit.criterion: Engineer-centric analogies and interaction cues (pipelines, backtesting, templates)
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks for the use of familiar engineering metaphors (e.g., pipeline, backtest, thresholds), and inclusion of reproducible templates or outline-like snippets that map the plan to the user’s tool-driven workflow, enhancing cognitive alignment.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Instrument-level, purchasable portfolio with weights and entry/rebalance rules
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the report specifies exact asset classes and concrete instruments (e.g., CN ETFs/fund codes/QDII), target weights, entry method (lump-sum vs DCA), and rebalancing thresholds/schedule so the user can buy immediately.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Explicit risk management rules with numeric triggers and contingencies
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses if the report defines measurable guardrails (e.g., max drawdown limits, single-position/sector caps, drift thresholds) and prescribes specific actions when breached (de-risking, reducing exposures), matching a conservative profile with a small high-risk sleeve.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Performance tracking and evaluation toolkit (KPIs, benchmarks, formulas/tools)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report provides step-by-step methods to compute TWR and XIRR, compare to relevant benchmarks, and track risk (volatility, max drawdown, Sharpe), with instructions for Excel/Python or retail apps and a review cadence (e.g., monthly).

actionability_practicality.criterion: Operational execution feasibility (platforms, accounts, fees, liquidity)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks that execution steps are practical: which domestic brokers/fund platforms to use, trading hours, KYC, purchase steps, fees (expense ratios, commissions, stamp duty), and liquidity checks so costs and slippage don’t derail the plan.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Quantified objectives and constraints for the six-month horizon
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies the annualized ≥10% goal is translated into a six-month target (approx. 4.9% net of fees) and paired with explicit constraints (e.g., minimum cash buffer, target volatility range), enabling measurable decision-making and expectation management.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Market adaptation rules with clear signals and actions
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the report includes simple, testable triggers (e.g., volatility or moving-average regimes, valuation bands) and prescribes precise adjustments (e.g., shift X% from equity ETF to bond/MMF), suitable for retail access without complex derivatives.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Time/automation suitability and adherence support
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks if the plan can be maintained with limited time via SIPs/auto-invest, threshold rebalancing, and optional Python/Excel templates or APIs, aligning with the user’s busy schedule and technical skills to reduce behavior gaps.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Localization and regulatory accessibility in mainland China
actionability_practicality.explanation: Ensures recommended instruments are available to CN retail investors and avoids reliance on products needing special permissions (margin/short/options) unless accessible alternatives are provided, making the plan legally and practically executable.

depth_insight.criterion: Feasibility and Risk–Return Calibration for the 6‑month ≥10% Annualized Goal
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the report quantifies the six‑month target (~4.9%) against realistic expected returns/volatility of candidate assets, estimates probability of success/shortfall, and frames required risk exposure and drawdown trade‑offs rather than relying on naive assumptions.

depth_insight.criterion: Depth of Personalization and Translation of Investor Profile into Constraints and Risk Budgets
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the report elicits or sensibly infers risk tolerance, liquidity/tax constraints, drawdown limits, time horizon nuances, and converts them into actionable risk budgets, allocation bounds, and guardrails that shape the portfolio.

depth_insight.criterion: Asset Allocation Framework Sophistication
depth_insight.explanation: Rates the insightfulness of the allocation logic across asset classes and factors (e.g., equity beta, duration, credit, value/quality, commodities), correlation-aware diversification, regime sensitivity, and use of principled methods (risk parity, Black–Litterman, heuristics with rationale) tailored to a short horizon.

depth_insight.criterion: Portfolio Construction and Instrument Selection Rigor
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses specificity and value of instrument choices (e.g., low-cost ETFs, T‑bill ladders, option overlays, structured products), position sizing, expected contribution to reaching the target, and implementability considering the six‑month window.

depth_insight.criterion: Risk Management Design and Downside Control Depth
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates identification of key risks (market, concentration, liquidity, tail) and the robustness of controls: drawdown limits, stop‑loss/position limits, hedges/collars, diversification, risk metrics (VaR/CVaR), and scenario/stress testing to manage shortfall risk over six months.

depth_insight.criterion: Monitoring, Performance Measurement, and Feedback Mechanisms
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the report defines appropriate benchmarks, return calculation methods (TWR vs MWR), KPIs (volatility, max drawdown, Sharpe/Sortino, hit rate), rebalancing and review cadences, triggers for action, and attribution to diagnose deviations and adapt.

depth_insight.criterion: Scenario/Regime Analysis and Contingency Planning
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates forward‑looking thinking across plausible six‑month market regimes (risk‑on/off, rate shifts, volatility spikes) and the presence of pre‑planned adjustments or tilts, showing preparedness for market changes.

depth_insight.criterion: Insight into Costs, Taxes, and Liquidity Frictions
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses analysis of expense ratios, bid‑ask spreads, slippage, short‑term tax treatment, and liquidity constraints that can materially affect outcomes over a short horizon and influence product selection and turnover.

depth_insight.criterion: Originality and Actionable Value of Recommendations
depth_insight.explanation: Rates whether the report offers differentiated yet prudent tactics (e.g., barbell safe yield plus convex upside, defined‑outcome ETFs, dynamic hedges) that enhance probability of meeting the target without excessive risk.

logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the report follows a coherent sequence tailored to the task: investor profiling and constraints → objective translation for a 6-month horizon → strategic asset allocation → product/instrument selection → implementation plan → monitoring and rebalancing rules. A complete and properly ordered framework prevents gaps and circular reasoning.

logical_coherence.criterion: Rigor of the Premise-to-Portfolio Reasoning Chain
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether the report explicitly translates the annualized 10% goal into a 6-month target, integrates risk tolerance and liquidity/time constraints, and logically derives asset-class weights and position sizing from these premises rather than asserting allocations. The conclusion (portfolio) must be a necessary consequence of stated premises.

logical_coherence.criterion: Alignment of Arguments with Supporting Evidence and Assumptions
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether key claims (e.g., expected returns, volatility, correlations, liquidity suitability over six months) are supported by relevant assumptions or references, and whether those assumptions are stated and used consistently in later decisions. Avoids unsupported assertions and ensures parameter choices are justified.

logical_coherence.criterion: Coherence of Risk Identification and Control Design
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks if identified risks (market, credit, duration, liquidity, FX, concentration) are explicitly mapped to specific controls (diversification, hedging, position limits, stop-loss/drawdown limits, duration/credit caps) and whether these controls are consistent with the portfolio and six-month horizon. Controls should logically mitigate the stated risks without contradicting the allocation logic.

logical_coherence.criterion: Monitoring, Benchmarking, and Feedback Loop Logic
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether performance tracking (e.g., time-weighted return vs. goal-converted 6-month target, benchmark selection, risk metrics like drawdown/volatility) is logically tied to the initial objectives and constraints, with clear review cadence and action triggers that feed back into rebalancing or de-risking. Ensures a closed-loop system rather than one-off recommendations.

logical_coherence.criterion: Smoothness of Transitions and Explicit Cross-Referencing
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether each section naturally follows from and references the prior one (e.g., product selection cites the chosen asset allocation; monitoring references stated targets/constraints), avoiding logical jumps. Smooth transitions help maintain the integrity of the reasoning flow.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structure, Navigation, and Layout Readability
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report uses a clear section hierarchy (e.g., objectives, risk profile assumptions, asset allocation, product selection, risk controls, implementation steps, monitoring plan), consistent headings/numbering, white space, and bullets; includes a table of contents or section links so readers can locate each part of the plan quickly.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity and Precision of Language
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether the text uses plain, concise sentences, avoids ambiguity and promotional tone, employs active voice, and expresses magnitudes and timeframes unambiguously (e.g., annualized vs 6‑month returns), enabling non-expert readers to understand instructions without misinterpretation.

clarity_readability.criterion: Terminology Usage and Definitions
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether financial terms (e.g., annualized return, CAGR, volatility/standard deviation, Sharpe, max drawdown, beta, correlation, expense ratio/TER, rebalancing bands, stop-loss, VaR) are used consistently, defined at first use or via a brief glossary, and kept to necessary jargon to lower comprehension barriers.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity of Tables and Numeric Presentation
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether portfolio and risk data are presented in well-labeled tables with consistent units and rounding (e.g., %, bps, currency), clear columns (weights, target ranges, instruments/tickers, fees, liquidity), footnotes for assumptions and data sources, and explicit time horizons so readers can read numbers at a glance.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Charts and Visualizations
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether charts (e.g., allocation bars, historical/illustrative performance lines, drawdown plots) are legible with labeled axes/units, clear legends, colorblind-safe palettes, readable fonts, concise captions stating the takeaway, and are referenced in text without clutter or misleading 3D effects.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Takeaways and Disclosures
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether executive summary, key points (target return, risk limits, rebalancing thresholds), and risk disclosures are surfaced via callouts, bolding, or bullet lists without over-highlighting; ensures important constraints and warnings are easy to spot and remember.

clarity_readability.criterion: Step-by-Step Implementation and Monitoring Checklist Readability
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether execution and tracking are presented as numbered steps and checklists (e.g., order placement, position sizing, rebalancing cadence, thresholds, KPIs like tracking error/variance, reporting templates), with clear sequencing and unambiguous instructions so the reader can follow the process smoothly.

task: I plan to make personal investments over the next six months with the goal of achieving an annualized return of at least 10%. I have no specific preference for the investment field or product type, which could include stocks, mutual funds, or other financial instruments. At the same time, I want to understand asset allocation strategies to better diversify risk and implement effective risk management during the investment process. Please provide me with targeted advice, including a personalized investment portfolio recommendation, as well as methods for tracking and evaluating my investment performance to ensure it aligns with my actual risk tolerance and market changes. I would like your professional guidance to help me plan my investments and achieve my financial objectives.

goal_alignment.criterion: Goal–horizon–risk coherence with probabilistic framing
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan explicitly converts the 10% annualized goal to a six-month target (~4.9%), quantifies expected return/volatility, sets a risk budget (e.g., acceptable max drawdown), and provides probability ranges under a moderate risk profile. This matches his analytical style and prevents overreach within a short horizon.

goal_alignment.criterion: Human-capital and tech-concentration-aware asset allocation
goal_alignment.explanation: Check if the allocation reduces correlation to his tech-heavy career and angel bets by using diversifying assets (quality/value/low-beta factors, bonds, commodities, global exposures), explicitly acknowledging his embedded tech beta. This addresses a key implicit risk unique to him.

goal_alignment.criterion: Liquidity, runway, and liability-aware design
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan carves out emergency and operating buffers (family + startup runway, mortgage payments), avoids illiquid lockups, and plans for travel periods. This aligns with his family obligations, mortgage, and variable cash flows, lowering financial and psychological stress.

goal_alignment.criterion: Quantitative risk management with explicit safeguards
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess inclusion of numeric guardrails (max drawdown limits, stop-loss or hedging rules, volatility-targeted sizing), stress tests (tech selloff, rate/rmb shocks), and downside metrics (VaR/expected shortfall). This fits his data-driven decision style and need for disciplined risk control.

goal_alignment.criterion: Implementable instrument mapping for a Beijing-based investor
goal_alignment.explanation: Check whether recommendations map to accessible, low-cost vehicles (e.g., A-share ETFs, domestic bond funds, QDII global funds/ETFs, money-market funds), consider currency risk, and outline practical execution. This ensures personalization to his investing context and feasibility.

goal_alignment.criterion: Adaptive rebalancing and regime-aware adjustments
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan defines clear, testable rules tied to market regimes (e.g., trend filters, volatility bands, credit-spread thresholds) with a low-maintenance cadence (e.g., monthly) that suits his schedule, enabling alignment with changing markets and his risk tolerance.

goal_alignment.criterion: KPI-driven performance tracking and automation in his workflow
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether it specifies benchmarks, TWR/IRR, Sharpe, max drawdown, hit rates, and provides an automated tracking setup (Notion dashboards, Sheets formulas/APIs, Slack alerts, decision logs). This matches his toolset (GitHub/Slack/Notion), analytical habits, and time efficiency.

goal_alignment.criterion: Concise, data-first communication with options and trade-offs
goal_alignment.explanation: Check that the report uses structured frameworks (e.g., core–satellite), tables/charts, and presents alternative portfolios with trade-offs so he can decide independently while leveraging team input. This aligns with his cognitive style and limited attention.

goal_alignment.criterion: Costs, taxes, and compliance/operational friction awareness
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan addresses expense ratios, trading costs, stamp duty, capital gains/tax treatment, and QDII/process constraints, favoring low-turnover, low-friction implementation that reduces maintenance burden and aligns with his family/work balance.

content_alignment.criterion: Entrepreneur Human-Capital and Tech-Concentration Offset in Portfolio Content
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the report explicitly recognizes his significant exposure to AI/tech via startup equity and angel investments, and thus proposes allocations that deliberately diversify away from domestic tech beta and illiquidity (e.g., caps on tech sector, offsets via bonds/commodities/defensives), with reasoning tied to his personal balance sheet.

content_alignment.criterion: China-Specific Investable Universe, Access Pathways, and Cost Sensitivity
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether instruments and examples are tailored to a Beijing-based investor: A-share/STAR/CSI ETFs, bond funds, money market funds, C-REITs, gold/commodity ETFs, Stock Connect usage, and QDII public funds for offshore exposure, including RMB/FX considerations and local fees/expense ratios to match his cost-effectiveness preference.

content_alignment.criterion: Advanced Quantitative Depth Matching High Financial Literacy
content_alignment.explanation: Check for use of risk-adjusted metrics (Sharpe/Sortino), rolling drawdowns, factor exposures (e.g., China/tech factor, value/momentum/quality), scenario/regime analysis, and volatility targeting, avoiding beginner-level explanations and generic tips that would under-serve his analytical capability.

content_alignment.criterion: Data-Driven Monitoring and Workflow Integration
content_alignment.explanation: Determine whether performance tracking and evaluation methods align with his GitHub/Slack/Notion and Python-centric habits—e.g., API-fed dashboards, automated alerts, scheduled data pulls, and reproducible metrics—so the content selection fits his established analytical workflow.

content_alignment.criterion: Six-Month Horizon–Appropriate Instrument and Rebalance Design
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the content tailors the asset mix and rebalancing cadence to a six-month window—favoring liquid vehicles, controlled turnover, appropriate bond duration, and realistic short-horizon tilts—rather than long-horizon or illiquid strategies misaligned with his timeframe.

content_alignment.criterion: Risk Management Framing Consistent with Moderate Risk Appetite
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the content selects risk controls consistent with moderate tolerance—e.g., explicit drawdown thresholds, position sizing limits, and hedging choices that avoid aggressive leverage or speculative derivatives—so the risk content fits his stated preferences.

content_alignment.criterion: Diversification Across Low-Correlation Sleeves with Quantified Rationale
content_alignment.explanation: Check that diversification is not generic: the report should propose sleeves (e.g., gov/credit bonds, commodities/gold, REITs, cash-like funds) and show how correlations/volatilities contribute to total risk reduction, aligning with his desire to understand asset allocation strategies.

content_alignment.criterion: Liquidity and Cash-Reserve Considerations for Family and Business Needs
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the content includes an explicit liquidity sleeve/emergency fund and prioritizes easy-to-liquidate holdings that reflect his mortgage, dependents, and entrepreneurial cashflow uncertainty, matching his life context and preference for manageable monitoring load.

presentation_fit.criterion: Structured executive summary and layered information architecture
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks for a TL;DR with key targets/assumptions followed by modular sections (allocation, portfolio, risk controls, tracking) and optional appendices, enabling quick skim then deeper dive for a time-constrained technical reader.

presentation_fit.criterion: Quantitative presentation clarity (KPIs, definitions, and consistency)
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether core metrics (e.g., annualized return, volatility, Sharpe, max drawdown, tracking vs benchmark) are foregrounded with concise definitions/units and consistent horizons (6-month vs annualized), suiting a data-driven decision style.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology precision and explanation balance
presentation_fit.explanation: Assesses whether finance/quant terms are used accurately with brief inline clarifications on first use, avoiding fluff or over-simplification, matching a financially savvy, technical audience.

presentation_fit.criterion: Visualization suitability and readability for portfolio and risk
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks for appropriate charts (allocation pie/bar, risk–return scatter, drawdown curve, performance vs benchmark, scenario tables) with clear labels, legends, colorblind-safe palette, and highlighted 10% target, improving rapid insight extraction.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tool-friendly formatting (Notion/Sheets/Slack-ready)
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether tables, checklists, and formula/code blocks are copy-pasteable, with clean headings and numbering, enabling seamless use in the user's common tools without reformatting.

presentation_fit.criterion: Navigation and cognitive load management
presentation_fit.explanation: Assesses presence of a table of contents, section numbering, callout boxes for assumptions/alerts, concise paragraphs, and bullet lists that reduce effort and speed reading.

presentation_fit.criterion: Localization of units and conventions
presentation_fit.explanation: Checks for RMB currency notation, appropriate date/number formats, and benchmark naming conventions relevant to the user's context, avoiding friction caused by mismatched formats.

presentation_fit.criterion: Risk communication formatting and placement
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether risk warnings, scenario labels (bull/base/bear), and key assumptions are visually separated and consistently formatted, keeping critical caveats visible without disrupting flow.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Concrete, executable portfolio specification accessible in Mainland China
actionability_practicality.explanation: The report should provide precise target weights, instrument names/tickers (A‑share/ETF/mutual fund/QDII), a manageable number of positions, and purchase channels available to a Beijing-based retail investor. Without instrument-level specificity and local accessibility, the user cannot act.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Quantified risk controls and position sizing rules
actionability_practicality.explanation: Provide numerical limits and rules (e.g., max position %, sector caps, volatility/VAR-based sizing, stop-loss or max drawdown thresholds, and cash buffer ranges). These translate risk management intent into enforceable actions aligned with the user’s moderate risk appetite.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Performance tracking and benchmarking framework with calculable metrics
actionability_practicality.explanation: Define benchmarks (e.g., blended indices), KPIs (time-weighted return, Sharpe, max drawdown, hit rate), calculation methods, and review frequency, plus practical tooling (e.g., broker exports to Sheets/Notion). Measurable tracking is essential to judge progress toward the 10% annualized target.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Rule-based rebalancing and dynamic adjustment triggers
actionability_practicality.explanation: Specify when and how to rebalance (e.g., drift >5% or quarterly), and adaptation rules tied to volatility regimes or drawdown thresholds. Clear triggers reduce ad-hoc decisions and keep the portfolio aligned with both market changes and evolving risk tolerance.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Liquidity and cash management fit for an entrepreneur
actionability_practicality.explanation: Include an emergency fund, target cash slice, and instrument liquidity (T+ settlement, redemption windows, avoiding lockups). This ensures the user can meet business/family cash needs and avoid forced sales, crucial over a six-month deployment.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Cost and tax practicality with net-of-fee estimates
actionability_practicality.explanation: Account for expense ratios, trading fees, stamp duties/taxes where applicable, and slippage; provide tactics to minimize costs (e.g., ETFs over higher-fee funds). The 10% target must be assessed on a net basis to be realistically actionable.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Operational execution plan and timeline
actionability_practicality.explanation: Offer a step-by-step checklist (account/broker, order types, staging buys, schedule), time commitment estimates, and automation options the user can follow given a busy schedule. Execution clarity determines whether the plan can be implemented promptly and correctly.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Target feasibility and scenario/contingency planning
actionability_practicality.explanation: Provide expected return/volatility ranges, base/bear/bull scenarios over six months, and pre-defined actions if performance deviates (e.g., cut risk if drawdown >X%). This grounds the 10% annualized goal in realistic outcomes and maps deviations to concrete responses.

depth_insight.criterion: Feasibility and Risk–Return Realism of the 10% Annualized Target
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the report translates the 10% annualized target into a six-month objective (~4.9%), estimates expected return/volatility of proposed portfolios, quantifies probability of success, and discusses required Sharpe/drawdown tradeoffs and path risk over a short horizon.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization via Risk Profile to Policy Portfolio Mapping
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates depth in diagnosing risk willingness, capacity, and need (e.g., drawdown limits, liquidity needs) and converting them into a policy allocation and explicit risk budget (volatility or max drawdown) rather than generic risk labels.

depth_insight.criterion: Portfolio Construction Depth (Diversification Mechanics and Factor Exposures)
depth_insight.explanation: Judges whether asset class and factor choices (equities, bonds, cash, alternatives; value/quality/momentum; duration) are justified with correlation/variance reasoning, contribution-to-risk analysis, and avoidance of hidden concentration.

depth_insight.criterion: Risk Management Framework and Risk Budgeting
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses sophistication of position sizing (e.g., volatility targeting, risk parity, capped position risk), drawdown controls, hedging (e.g., collars), rebalancing bands, and their tradeoffs and limitations in a six-month context.

depth_insight.criterion: Scenario/Regime Planning and Stress Testing
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the report considers plausible market regimes (risk-on/off, rate shocks, recession), performs historical/stylized stress tests, and specifies contingent actions or tilts tied to observable indicators.

depth_insight.criterion: Implementation and Product Selection Insight
depth_insight.explanation: Looks at the practicality of instrument choices (ETFs, mutual funds, T-bills, options) including costs, liquidity, tracking error, tax considerations, and currency hedging, adding implementable value beyond abstract allocation.

depth_insight.criterion: Performance Tracking, Benchmarks, and Diagnostic Attribution
depth_insight.explanation: Rates the rigor of monitoring: appropriate benchmarks/policy index, choice of TWR vs MWR, risk-adjusted metrics (Sharpe/Sortino, max drawdown), tracking error, and attribution (allocation/selection/timing) with decision rules for adjustments.

depth_insight.criterion: Originality and Actionable Edge Beyond Generic Advice
depth_insight.explanation: Rewards non-obvious, implementable ideas (e.g., barbell with T-bills plus factor tilts, option collars targeting downside, probability-based target setting) that materially improve the plan’s efficacy or risk control.

logical_coherence.explanation: Logical coherence begins with clearly stated premises (10% annualized target, six-month horizon, risk tolerance, liquidity/constraints) and visible alignment: later recommendations must explicitly reference and honor these inputs.

Stating assumptions (expected returns/volatility/correlations, fees, taxes, rebalancing frequency) and applying them consistently prevents hidden leaps and ensures that calculations and conclusions are traceable and internally consistent.

logical_coherence.criterion: Deductive Mapping from Objectives to Strategic Asset Allocation and Instruments
logical_coherence.explanation: A stepwise derivation from objectives and risk profile to strategic allocation (e.g., core–satellite) and then to specific vehicles (stocks, funds, bonds, alternatives) shows that the portfolio is reasoned, not asserted.

logical_coherence.criterion: Feasibility and Risk–Return Consistency of the Target
logical_coherence.explanation: Converting the 10% annualized goal to a six-month benchmark and testing it against expected returns, drawdowns, and the investor’s risk tolerance ensures that the target and the plan are logically compatible.

Each key recommendation (allocation weights, instrument choice, risk limits) should cite relevant data or standard models/metrics, with a clear link from evidence to claim, avoiding unsupported assertions.
Transitions should connect outputs to inputs across sections (objectives → allocation → instrument selection → risk controls → implementation → monitoring), avoiding logical gaps or abrupt jumps.

Position sizing, entry/exit, rebalancing, and risk limits must lead to monitoring metrics and decision rules that feed back into adjustments as markets or risk tolerance change, ensuring a closed logical loop from plan to adaptation.

clarity_readability.criterion: Task-tailored Structure and Navigability of Formatting/Layout
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report has a clean, scannable layout with a table of contents, clear heading hierarchy, and consistent styles organized around the investor workflow (goals, risk profiling, asset allocation, product selection, implementation, monitoring). White space, bullet points, and section summaries should make it easy to locate the portfolio recommendation and the tracking plan for the six-month horizon.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity, Precision, and Readability of Language
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether sentences are concise and unambiguous, terms are used consistently (e.g., annualized vs six-month return), and the tone is professional yet accessible. Focuses on grammar, active voice, and avoiding vague qualifiers so readers can quickly understand instructions and caveats without misreading intent.

clarity_readability.criterion: Definition and User-friendly Explanation of Technical Terms and Metrics
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether financial jargon and metrics are clearly introduced and briefly explained at first use (e.g., CAGR/annualized return, volatility, max drawdown, Sharpe, beta, tracking error, rebalancing bands, stop-loss), with intuitive descriptions or micro-examples. A mini-glossary or callouts should lower the barrier for non-expert readers.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness and Integrity of Data, Charts, and Visualizations
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks that charts/tables are well-chosen and legible (allocation pie/bar, risk–return scatter, drawdown curve, performance vs benchmark), with clear titles, axes, units, legends, and color-blind-friendly palettes. Annotations should highlight the 10% annualized target and monitoring thresholds, and captions should state the key takeaway without requiring guesswork.

clarity_readability.criterion: Numerical Presentation, Units, and Assumptions Transparency
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether numbers use consistent units/time bases (six-month vs annualized clearly reconciled), and whether assumptions (fees, taxes, compounding method, benchmark choice, data date) are plainly stated via footnotes or callouts. Tables should have clear column labels, sensible rounding, and avoid mixing gross/net returns without explicit labels.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Takeaways and Actionable Steps
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether the report surfaces critical points via an executive summary, callout boxes, and checklists (e.g., allocation snapshot, rebalancing triggers, monitoring cadence, KPI dashboard). A brief end-summary should restate what to do next so the reader can implement and track without wading through dense text.

task: I plan to make personal investments over the next six months with the goal of achieving an annualized return of at least 10%. I have no specific preference for the investment field or product type, which could include stocks, mutual funds, or other financial instruments. At the same time, I want to understand asset allocation strategies to better diversify risk and implement effective risk management during the investment process. Please provide me with targeted advice, including a personalized investment portfolio recommendation, as well as methods for tracking and evaluating my investment performance to ensure it aligns with my actual risk tolerance and market changes. I would like your professional guidance to help me plan my investments and achieve my financial objectives.

goal_alignment.criterion: Return target–time horizon–risk tolerance reconciliation
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the article explicitly analyzes the feasibility of achieving an annualized 10% target over a six-month window for a moderate-risk, mainland investor, quantifies expected return/volatility ranges, and sets acceptable max drawdown limits. This aligns his cautious style and family obligations with the ambitious target to avoid misaligned risk-taking.

goal_alignment.criterion: China-accessible personalized asset allocation with explicit weights
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the portfolio uses onshore, easily accessible RMB instruments (e.g., broad equity ETFs/index funds, high-grade bond funds, money market/bank WMPs) with clear percentage weights and rationale tied to his moderate risk tolerance and low-cost preference. Concrete weights enable execution by a busy manager and match his cost-effectiveness mindset.

goal_alignment.criterion: Actionable risk management and drawdown control
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for practical rules on position sizing, maximum single-asset exposure, portfolio max drawdown thresholds, rebalancing bands, and simple hedging or low-volatility tilts suitable for a non-day-trader. This caters to his stability priority and limited time while reducing path risk during the six-month horizon.

goal_alignment.criterion: Liquidity planning and family cash-flow safeguards
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan sets an emergency fund, earmarks liquidity for near-term expenses (e.g., summer trip, family needs), and avoids locking capital in illiquid or complex products. This reflects his family-centric responsibilities and desire for steady, low-stress investing.

goal_alignment.criterion: Tracking and evaluation framework aligned to routines/tools
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the article provides a concise monitoring dashboard (e.g., annualized return vs target, volatility, max drawdown, benchmark comparison), a weekly or biweekly morning review cadence, and implementation via common Chinese finance apps/WeChat reminders. This matches his morning planning habit and practical tech usage.

goal_alignment.criterion: Cost discipline and fee/tax awareness
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate emphasis on low expense ratios, minimized transaction costs, and basic awareness of fee structures for domestic funds/ETFs and bank WMPs. This fits his cost-effectiveness preference and ensures net returns are aligned with the 10% target.

goal_alignment.criterion: Scenario analysis and expectation management
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for base/upside/downside scenarios over six months, with probability-informed ranges, sensitivity to market shocks, and contingency actions if performance deviates. This supports his rational, engineering mindset and helps align goals with market realities.

goal_alignment.criterion: Engineering-friendly communication and structure
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the content is structured (objectives–constraints–allocation–rules–execution–review), uses clear numbers and checklists, and avoids unnecessary jargon. This caters to his analytical background and desire for practical, step-by-step guidance.

goal_alignment.criterion: Family involvement and behavioral safeguards
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan includes pre-commitment rules (e.g., cooling-off periods, rule-based rebalancing), and recommends family consultation for major allocation shifts. This aligns with his decision style (independent yet consultative) and reduces emotional errors.

goal_alignment.criterion: Local market suitability and regulatory fit
goal_alignment.explanation: Check that recommended instruments and processes are realistically accessible to a Harbin-based mainland investor, avoiding unsuitable leveraged/complex products and acknowledging domestic market practices. This ensures practical, compliant implementation.

content_alignment.criterion: Risk-Tolerance-Calibrated Content and Drawdown Framing
content_alignment.explanation: Checks whether the report explicitly tailors allocation and risk controls to a moderate-risk profile while contextualizing the 10% annualized target (e.g., expected volatility/drawdown ranges, position limits, avoidance of leveraged/opaque products), matching the user’s cautious temperament and real tolerance.

content_alignment.criterion: Mainland China Instrument and Access Localization
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether product choices and benchmarks are RMB-denominated and realistically accessible to a Mainland investor (A-share ETFs/LOFs, bond and money market funds, bank WMPs, C-REITs, QDII funds) with China-relevant context, instead of foreign-only tickers or inaccessible platforms.

content_alignment.criterion: Family Cash-Flow and Liquidity Integration
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether the content embeds emergency reserves and short-term liquidity for foreseeable needs (child education, family trips, parental support) and aligns with his monthly budgeting habit, ensuring the allocation reflects his household responsibilities.

content_alignment.criterion: Tracking Metrics, Benchmarks, and Review Cadence Tailored to Habits
content_alignment.explanation: Checks if performance monitoring is built around tools he uses (finance apps/WeChat mini programs) and his morning planning routine, using appropriate metrics (e.g., XIRR/TWR, max drawdown) and RMB benchmarks (e.g., CSI 300 + ChinaBond Aggregate) with a monthly review cadence.

content_alignment.criterion: Time Horizon Staging and Core–Satellite Structure
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether the content distinguishes the six-month plan from longer-term goals (retirement/education) via buckets or a core–satellite framework, and sets a rebalancing frequency that fits his schedule and moderate risk posture.

content_alignment.criterion: Diversification Across Accessible Asset Classes and Regions
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether diversification spans domestic equities, high-quality bonds/cash, and selective overseas exposure via QDII, using instruments realistically usable in Mainland China and appropriate for a moderate investor.

content_alignment.criterion: Engineering-Minded Depth and Quantitative Clarity
content_alignment.explanation: Checks that explanations use structured, data-aware reasoning (e.g., risk/return ranges, simple formulas, scenario bands) at an intermediate depth suitable for an engineering-trained reader, avoiding both superficiality and excessive jargon.

content_alignment.criterion: Cost Efficiency and Fee Sensitivity in Product Selection
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether the content emphasizes low-cost vehicles (index funds/ETFs) and fee awareness consistent with his cost-effectiveness preference, ensuring product choices align with his shopping and budgeting mindset.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tone and register fit for a Mainland Chinese professional reader
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the language is calm, professional, respectful (e.g., using “您”), and non-promotional, matching a rational SOE manager’s expectations and avoiding slang or hype.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology clarity and localization
presentation_fit.explanation: Check if standard Mainland finance terms are used and briefly explained inline (e.g., definitions for annualized return, volatility, max drawdown), minimizing jargon load while maintaining precision.

presentation_fit.criterion: Layered structure and scannability
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether the report offers an executive summary and clear sections aligned to decision flow (goals → risk profile → allocation → implementation → monitoring), using headings, numbered steps, and key takeaways for quick morning reading.

presentation_fit.criterion: Visualization appropriateness and labeling
presentation_fit.explanation: Judge whether charts are simple and purpose-fit (e.g., pie for allocation, scenario bands), with clear titles, legends, units, readable scales, and local color conventions (red=up/green=down).

presentation_fit.criterion: Numeric presentation and unit transparency
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess consistency and clarity of numbers (annualized vs six-month returns, % units, decimal precision), concise display of key formulas/assumptions, and avoidance of misleading precision.

presentation_fit.criterion: Mobile/WeChat-friendly layout
presentation_fit.explanation: Check if paragraphs are short, bullets are used, key figures are highlighted, and subheadings appear frequently to ensure easy reading and sharing on mobile devices.

presentation_fit.criterion: Process presentation via checklists and timelines
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether implementation, tracking, and rebalancing steps are presented as checklists or a six-month timeline/calendar, enhancing followability without changing content.

presentation_fit.criterion: Risk and uncertainty framing style
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess if uncertainty is communicated with balanced, neutral language and simple scenario visuals (base/optimistic/pessimistic), with concise disclaimers that do not disrupt flow.

presentation_fit.criterion: Personalization and relatable analogies (used sparingly)
presentation_fit.explanation: Check for polite address (e.g., using the user’s name/“您”) and occasional analogies aligned with interests (chess/photography) to aid comprehension without distracting from clarity.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Concrete, executable portfolio (products, weights, and trade rules)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the report specifies exact investable instruments available to Mainland retail investors (e.g., broad-market ETFs, open-end funds), target weights, initial funding plan, order type guidance, and a simple rebalancing schedule so the user can place trades immediately.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Return target realism and assumptions for a 6-month window
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the 10% annualized target is translated into a 6-month objective (~4.9–5% nominal), with expected return ranges, drawdown expectations, and assumption transparency (e.g., historical ranges or scenario analysis) to support informed go/no-go decisions.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Risk management rules and guardrails
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses the presence of implementable risk controls: max drawdown threshold and actions, position size and single-asset caps, equity allocation bounds, no leverage/margin, and ring-fencing an emergency fund so family finances are protected.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Liquidity and lock-up suitability for a 6-month horizon
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks that recommended products have daily or T+1/T+2 liquidity, minimal redemption fees, and no lock-ups, enabling exits or reallocations if market conditions or family needs change within six months.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Implementation steps and channel feasibility in Mainland China
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies that the report gives step-by-step execution guidance (account opening/KYC, platform suggestions such as bank/broker/Alipay/Eastmoney, trading hours/settlement basics) with realistic time estimates, ensuring the plan can actually be set up.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Monitoring, evaluation metrics, and rebalance triggers
actionability_practicality.explanation: Looks for a clear review cadence (e.g., weekly glance, monthly review), concrete metrics (time-weighted return vs. benchmark, max drawdown, volatility), tool/app or simple spreadsheet guidance, and drift/risk thresholds that trigger rebalancing.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Cost and tax transparency with net-of-fee expectations
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether fees (fund TERs, brokerage commissions, stamp duty where applicable, redemption fees) are estimated and incorporated into net return expectations, with cost-minimization tips to keep the 10% goal realistic after costs.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Risk-tolerance translation into allocation choices
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the report provides model variants or risk bands (e.g., conservative/base/aggressive) with expected drawdowns so the user can select and adjust an allocation aligned to his moderate risk appetite.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Predefined market-change playbook
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report defines specific actions for major market moves or macro shifts (e.g., if equity index drops/rises by X%, or rates move by Y bps), using simple instruments, to reduce ad-hoc, emotional decisions.

depth_insight.criterion: Goal Feasibility and Risk–Return Trade-off Analysis for a 6-Month Horizon
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the report quantifies what a 10% annualized target implies over six months (required return, volatility, drawdown risk), uses base rates across asset mixes, and evaluates probability of success and trade-offs versus risk tolerance.

depth_insight.criterion: Depth of Asset Allocation Design and Diversification Rationale
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the allocation is built from evidence-based expected returns, risks, and correlations, includes factor/asset-class diversification, and justifies strategic core vs tactical tilts or regime sensitivity to pursue the target while managing risk.

depth_insight.criterion: Sophistication of Risk Management Framework
depth_insight.explanation: Examines the richness and practicality of risk controls (position sizing, volatility targeting, max drawdown limits, hedging/collars, concentration and liquidity limits) tailored to a short investment horizon.

depth_insight.criterion: Performance Tracking, Attribution, and Adaptive Adjustment Methodology
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the depth of the monitoring system: appropriate benchmarks, time- vs money-weighted returns, risk-adjusted metrics (Sharpe/Sortino), drawdown tracking, attribution, drift/threshold rebalancing, and predefined triggers for adjustments as markets change.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization: Translating Risk Tolerance and Capacity into Portfolio Risk Parameters
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the report elicits and maps risk preference, capacity, and need into explicit portfolio targets (volatility bands, max drawdown, allocation ranges) and uses them to tailor the recommendation.

depth_insight.criterion: Implementation Insight: Product/Instrument Selection with Friction Awareness
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates how well the plan maps to concrete instruments (ETFs/funds/options) with attention to expense ratios, liquidity, tracking error, tax treatment (short-term gains), and operational feasibility over six months.

depth_insight.criterion: Use of Base Rates, Assumptions, and Scenario/Sensitivity Analysis
depth_insight.explanation: Checks whether assumptions on returns/volatility/correlation are explicit, sourced or reasonable, and whether the report tests outcomes under alternative market regimes and sensitivity to key inputs.

depth_insight.criterion: Original, Actionable Insights Tailored to Short-Horizon Investing
depth_insight.explanation: Rewards unique, high-value ideas beyond common advice (e.g., volatility targeting, options collars to cap drawdowns, cash management for dry powder, acknowledging sequence risk and tax frictions).

logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the report explicitly states and consistently references the investor’s premises (time horizon of six months, annualized 10% target, risk tolerance, liquidity/constraints) and uses them to anchor subsequent sections.

Evaluates whether the annualized 10% goal is logically translated into a six-month target and implied risk/volatility requirements, with clear reasoning about feasibility and trade-offs given the short horizon.

logical_coherence.criterion: Allocation Derivation from Risk Profile and Constraints
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether the asset allocation is derived through a transparent process (e.g., risk-budgeting, mean–variance, core–satellite) that maps stated risk tolerance and constraints to weights, rather than presenting arbitrary splits.

Checks that claims about expected returns, risks, correlations, and costs are tied to sources or stated assumptions and that timeframes and definitions are consistent with the six-month plan and annualized target.

logical_coherence.criterion: Product Selection Consistency with Allocation Logic
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether chosen instruments (e.g., ETFs, funds, bonds) logically implement the allocation rationale by matching required exposures, liquidity, cost, and risk characteristics appropriate for a six-month horizon.

Assesses the logical mapping from identified risks (market, concentration, liquidity, drawdown) to specific controls (position sizing, diversification, hedging, stop-loss) with explicit trigger conditions and decision rules.

logical_coherence.criterion: Performance Tracking Metrics, Benchmarks, and Feedback Loop
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether KPIs (e.g., annualized return vs. six-month target, drawdown, tracking error, risk-adjusted metrics) and benchmarks are defined and linked to review cadence, rebalancing thresholds, and course-correction logic.

Verifies that allocations sum to 100%, constraints are respected (liquidity, limits), definitions are consistent (annualized vs. period returns), and that there is no circular reasoning across sections.
Evaluates whether transitions guide the reader from premises to allocation to instruments to risk controls to monitoring without gaps or logical jumps, maintaining continuity of the reasoning chain.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structured Layout with Clear Sections and Hierarchy
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report uses a clean, consistent heading hierarchy (e.g., Objectives, Risk Profile, Asset Allocation, Implementation, Monitoring/Tracking, Adjustments) with logical section ordering, readable spacing, and scannable subsections so readers can quickly locate the parts relevant to portfolio setup and six‑month monitoring.

clarity_readability.criterion: Plain, Precise, and Concise Language (with Numeric Clarity)
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether sentences are straightforward and free of ambiguity; numbers use consistent units, currencies, and time bases (e.g., annualized vs. monthly, gross vs. net of fees), and thresholds (e.g., rebalancing bands) are stated unambiguously to prevent misinterpretation.

clarity_readability.criterion: Definition and Consistent Use of Financial Terms and Metrics
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether key terms (annualized return, volatility, Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, rebalancing band, target allocation, benchmark) are defined at first use or in a glossary, used consistently across the document, and accompanied by brief, reader‑friendly explanations.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Charts and Tables for Allocation and Performance
