presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluates whether recommended resources are listed with a uniform schema (e.g., title, author/vendor, focus, level, tags) and concise one-line descriptors, enabling rapid scanning aligned with the user’s rational, data-aware decision style.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Realistic 6‑month schedule and word‑count capacity fit
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the report converts 6 months and ~10 hours/week into a phased plan (outline, draft, revision) with explicit word‑count math (e.g., target words/hour, weekly totals), buffers for academic peaks, and reconciles the daily 1,500‑word preference with actual available hours.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Executable weekly/daily workflow design
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses if the plan breaks down each week and 2‑hour evening sessions into concrete tasks (e.g., scene drafting, beat mapping, character passes), includes time‑boxed sprints, checklists, and clear end‑of‑session deliverables so Xu Yang can immediately act.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Romance‑specific structure and pacing toolkit with diagnostics
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report provides actionable romance beat frameworks and scene‑level pacing methods (e.g., scene/sequel, micro‑tension, cliffhanger hooks) plus diagnostics (chapter length distribution, slow‑scene flags) to prevent drag and create suspense.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Actionable character relationship design framework
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for step‑by‑step tools (relationship arc map, conflict matrix, misbelief/backstory wound, chemistry beats) and exercises that lead to deep, coherent character dynamics aligned with the romance genre.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Scrivener‑centered tool workflow setup and reliability
actionability_practicality.explanation: Looks for a concrete Scrivener project setup (folders, metadata for POV/threads, labels, custom templates), compile settings, and backup/versioning steps (e.g., cloud/local) so tools directly support the plan without overhead.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Measurable progress tracking and iterative adjustment
actionability_practicality.explanation: Determines whether the plan defines KPIs (daily/weekly words, scenes completed, revision pages), provides tracking templates (Excel/Notion), and sets weekly reviews to diagnose slippage and adjust targets methodically.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Resource recommendations’ accessibility, cost, and integration
actionability_practicality.explanation: Verifies that recommended books/tools are easy to obtain in mainland China (e‑book or major platforms), are student‑budget friendly or have free alternatives, and are scheduled into the workflow with specific use cases.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Risk and contingency strategies (time conflicts, motivation, scope)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for concrete fallback plans: light weeks around exams, catch‑up buffers, micro‑goals for low‑energy days, anti‑scope‑creep practices (premise lock/change log), and methods to recover from missed sessions.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Practical revision and feedback pipeline within timeline
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the plan includes time‑boxed developmental and line‑edit passes with checklists, targeted beta feedback steps, and acceptance criteria to move from draft to finished manuscript within the 6 months.

depth_insight.criterion: Quantified Feasibility Modeling and Milestone Design
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan translates the 6‑month timeline, 10 hours/week, and 80k–150k goal into a realistic capacity model (words/hour, draft vs. revision split, buffers), with milestones and trade-offs justified by calculations rather than assumptions.

depth_insight.criterion: Romance-specific Character Relationship Architecture
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates depth of insight in designing relationship dynamics (e.g., complementary flaws/misbeliefs, power/agency balance, trope use/subversion, value conflicts) and integrating emotional arcs with plot beats to add genuine depth beyond generic character tips.

depth_insight.criterion: Suspense and Pacing Strategy Depth (Macro and Micro)
depth_insight.explanation: Judges whether the plan provides mechanisms to create and sustain tension (reveal cadence, stakes escalation, scene-level goals/conflicts, micro-tension diagnostics) and concrete tactics to avoid drag across acts and within scenes.

depth_insight.criterion: Risk Anticipation and High-leverage Mitigation/Feedback Loops
depth_insight.explanation: Looks for identification of likely bottlenecks (sagging middle, burnout, outline collapse) and embeds targeted mitigations (checkpoints, tension audits, beta reads, contingency buffers) that raise the plan’s robustness and insight value.

depth_insight.criterion: Tool and Workflow Leverage (Scrivener-centered) for Problem-solving
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether Scrivener (and complementary tools) are used strategically to solve the user’s specific craft and process problems (e.g., custom metadata to track tension/POV, corkboard for beat mapping, snapshots for iterative rewrites), not merely listed.

depth_insight.criterion: Curation of Resources with Application Guidance
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates the originality and utility of recommended books/tools and whether the report explains how and when to use them within the six-month plan to address romance craft, suspense, pacing, and first-novel challenges.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization to Writer’s Constraints and Motivation Mechanics
depth_insight.explanation: Judges whether the plan tailors targets and routines to the user’s schedule, prior short-form experience, and motivation preferences (e.g., calibrating daily word counts, weekend deep work, recovery and habit loops) with reasoning-backed adjustments.

logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether the report is organized around the user’s stated objectives (character development, plot, suspense, pacing) and constraints (6 months, 10 hours/week, 80k–150k words, daily target, Scrivener preference). A logically coherent plan should open with inputs/assumptions, define goals, lay out a phased methodology, specify milestones/monitoring, risk buffers, and resources—showing clear alignment throughout.

Checks that the report explicitly derives schedule and milestones from premises (e.g., daily/weekly word count to monthly totals; hours/week to drafting and revision allocations), verifies the cumulative totals reach 80k–150k within six months, and includes buffer/contingency logic. Arithmetic, assumptions, and any capacity trade-offs must be stated and internally consistent.

logical_coherence.criterion: Argument–Evidence Linkage for Methods and Recommendations
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether claims (e.g., adopting three-act/beat sheets, scene goals for pacing, specific suspense techniques, Scrivener for project management, selected craft books) are justified with relevant rationale or sources tied to the user’s goals and genre. Each recommended practice should be supported by context-appropriate reasoning rather than assertion.

logical_coherence.criterion: Sequential Logic and Dependency Management Across Plan Phases
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether tasks are ordered with correct dependencies (e.g., character/relationship design → outline/beat sheet → scene list → drafting → staged revisions → polish), that outputs from one phase feed inputs to the next, and that no circular or conflicting steps exist. Milestones should logically build on prior deliverables.

logical_coherence.criterion: Coherent Transitions and Signposting Between Sections
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks for explicit logical bridges (e.g., ‘Given the 10h/week limit, we allocate Xh to drafting and Yh to revision…’) that connect sections (inputs → plan design → schedule → monitoring → resources). Smooth transitions prevent reasoning gaps and help the reader follow how conclusions are reached from prior premises.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clean, Hierarchical Formatting and Layout
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report uses a clear heading hierarchy (e.g., Overview, Schedule, Character Development, Plot & Pacing, Tools, Reading List), consistent typography, adequate white space, and bullet/numbered lists so sections like the 6‑month timeline or daily targets are easy to scan and locate.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity, Concision, and Reader-Oriented Language
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether instructions are written in plain, unambiguous, and concise language with concrete verbs and measurable phrasing (e.g., “Write 1,500 words/day, 5 days/week”) and minimal hedging, enabling a beginner to understand exactly what to do without interpreting intent.

clarity_readability.criterion: Introduction and Consistent Use of Craft and Tool Terminology
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether writing-craft terms (e.g., inciting incident, beat sheet, scene/sequel, midpoint, stakes) and software jargon (e.g., Scrivener Binder, Corkboard, Compile, Snapshots) are defined on first use, used consistently, and, where helpful, briefly exemplified to reduce the learning curve.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Visual Aids (Timelines, Tables, Checklists, Diagrams)
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether visuals such as a 6‑month timeline, weekly schedule table, progress checklist, and character relationship map are legible, correctly labeled, captioned, and referenced in text; use accessible contrast/colors; and actually clarify the steps instead of duplicating or cluttering them.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Information and Navigation Aids
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses the presence and usefulness of an executive summary, quick-start checklist, section summaries, callouts for milestones and word-count targets, a table of contents/anchors, and consistent signposting that help readers quickly find and revisit critical instructions.

clarity_readability.criterion: Consistency of Units, Labels, and Notation
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether time and word-count units (e.g., hours/week, words/day), date formats, figure/table numbering, bullet styles, and naming conventions are consistent across the document to prevent confusion when following the plan week to week.

task: I hope to complete my first novel within the next six months, with a particular focus on character development and plot construction. The genre I plan to write in is romance, with a target word count of approximately 80,000 to 150,000 words. I already have some initial story ideas, such as the prototypes for the main characters and key plot points, but I want to design reasonable character relationships that add depth to them.I have previously written some short stories and essays but have not yet published any work. In terms of plot construction, I want to solve the problems of how to create suspense and avoid dragging the pacing. I can dedicate around 10 hours per week to writing—about 2 hours each weekday evening, with more concentrated writing on weekends. My preference is to set a daily word-count target (e.g., 1,500 words) to help maintain motivation. As for tools, I lean toward using professional writing software like Scrivener.Please design a writing plan for me, and also recommend relevant books and writing tools.

goal_alignment.criterion: Six-month roadmap realism aligned with Zhao Li’s schedule and energy
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan translates 10 hours/week into a phased timeline (discovery/outline, drafting, revision) with weekday 2-hour evening sessions, weekend deep-work blocks, travel buffers, and protection of her 10:30 PM bedtime and morning routines. A realistic cadence reduces burnout and increases completion odds for a first long-form novel.

goal_alignment.criterion: Psychology-driven character and relationship design
goal_alignment.explanation: Check if character building leverages her psychology minor (e.g., internal misbeliefs, attachment styles, love languages, Big Five traits, goal–wound–need) and maps relationship dynamics over the romance arc. This aligns with her strengths and directly addresses her desire to deepen character relationships.

goal_alignment.criterion: Romance-genre beats and emotional arc specificity
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan uses romance-specific structures (e.g., trope selection, meet-cute, midpoint shift, dark night, grand gesture, HEA/HFN) and aligns external plot with an escalating emotional intimacy arc. Genre-specific scaffolding ensures market-fit and clarity for a first-time romance novelist.

goal_alignment.criterion: Actionable pacing and suspense mechanics at scene level
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan teaches micro-tension, scene–sequel structure, hook/click-forward moments, strategic reveals, and subplot weaving, with checklists applied to her key plot points. This addresses her explicit need to create suspense and avoid dragging.

goal_alignment.criterion: Product-ops style process: OKRs, sprints, metrics, and retrospectives
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan frames goals as OKRs (e.g., milestone word counts, scene completion, revision passes), uses 1–2 week sprints, a feature backlog (scenes/threads), dashboards for word/time tracking, and end-of-sprint retros. This matches her professional habits and strengthens execution.

goal_alignment.criterion: Motivation and stress management tailored to low stress tolerance
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for flexible target design (e.g., time-based targets on weekdays, word-based on weekends), built-in recovery days, gentle fail-safes, accountability (buddy/group), ritualization (e.g., pre-write warmups, cat/tea ritual), and celebration milestones. Personalizing motivation safeguards consistency.

goal_alignment.criterion: Tool ecosystem fit and cost-conscious workflow centered on Scrivener
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether recommendations provide a Scrivener-based pipeline (templates for romance beats, character sheets), visual planners (Plottr/Milanote), timeline/outliner integrations, distraction blockers, cross-device/offline options for travel, and budget-aware alternatives/trials with setup steps.

goal_alignment.criterion: Social media distraction control and community/accountability leverage
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan proposes concrete boundaries (focus modes, scheduled check-ins) and uses Weibo/Xiaohongshu/podcasts/creative communities for progress logs, beta readers, and gentle audience-building without derailing writing time. This aligns with her online habits and need for support.

goal_alignment.criterion: Curated, prioritized learning plan integrated into mornings
goal_alignment.explanation: Check for a short, high-impact list of craft books and podcasts (CN/EN editions where possible) mapped to her issues (character depth, romance beats, pacing), with a weekly reading/listening schedule slotted into her 7–9 AM window. This leverages her morning preference and avoids overwhelm.

goal_alignment.criterion: Plug-and-play actionability: templates, checklists, and concrete examples
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan includes ready-to-use beat sheets, character relationship matrices, scene cards, sprint agendas, and pacing checklists tailored to romance, so she can start immediately with minimal cognitive load—important for a first novel and a busy schedule.

content_alignment.criterion: Psychologically Grounded Character Relationship Design
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the content leverages the user's psychology background to deepen romance dynamics (e.g., attachment styles, core misbeliefs, love languages, conflict/repair cycles) and offers nuanced methods for translating these concepts into arcs, scenes, and dialogue at an intermediate level.

content_alignment.criterion: Romance-Specific Suspense and Pacing Techniques at Intermediate Depth
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan targets suspense and pacing specifically within romance (secrets, miscommunication, escalating relational stakes, dual-POV reveals, scene–sequel rhythm, beat-level escalation) and goes beyond beginner tips, matching her prior short-form writing experience.

content_alignment.criterion: Use of Personal-Life Context as Story Material
content_alignment.explanation: Check if the content prompts and frameworks explicitly mine her Beijing/Nanjing background, urban routines (cafés, yoga, community gardens), travel, and pet (cat as catalyst) to build authentic settings, meet-cutes, motifs, and conflicts unique to her life.

content_alignment.criterion: Alignment with Word-Count-Based Productivity and Time Pattern
content_alignment.explanation: Determine if the plan aligns with her preference for daily word-count targets (e.g., 1,500 words), acknowledges a 10-hour/week capacity, and leverages morning focus and weekend deep work—fitting content choices to her actual schedule rather than generic timelines.

content_alignment.criterion: Tool Ecosystem Fit: Scrivener-Centric + Visual and Mobile Capture
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether recommendations prioritize Scrivener workflows (templates, collections), compatible planning tools (Plottr/Aeon Timeline), visual moodboards (e.g., Milanote/Notion boards for saved screenshots), mobile idea capture, and CN-friendly options where relevant.

content_alignment.criterion: Channel and Community Alignment (Podcasts/Long-Form + CN Platforms)
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether resources are offered in her preferred media formats (podcasts, long videos) and point to appropriate romance-writing communities/accountability options on Weibo/Xiaohongshu or similar local platforms she already uses.

content_alignment.criterion: Data-Driven, Iterative Development Mindset
content_alignment.explanation: Check if the content matches her product-ops mindset by including measurable progress metrics (draft velocity, scene length targets), feedback loops (beta readers/sensitivity readers), and structured iteration cycles (outline A/B, milestone reviews).

content_alignment.criterion: Stress-Aware Creative Process Elements
content_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan includes low-pressure mechanisms that respect her low stress tolerance (buffer weeks, mental-health-friendly rituals, gentle sprints, recovery days) without shifting into generic wellness advice.

content_alignment.criterion: Budget and Value Sensitivity in Books/Tools
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether recommendations reflect her rational, value-conscious spending—offering tiered options (free trials, library/ebook choices, cost-effective tools) and avoiding unnecessary paid stacks.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tone and readability match a young internet product-ops writer
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the report uses concise, supportive, second-person language with medium-length sentences, avoids shaming or overly academic prose, and maintains an encouraging, low-pressure tone suited to her low stress tolerance.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology calibration with brief definitions and bilingual cues
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that essential fiction-craft terms (e.g., POV, beats, scene/sequel, GMC) are used appropriately with first-mention glosses and Chinese-English mapping, avoiding unexplained jargon that could disrupt flow.

presentation_fit.criterion: Structure and navigation aligned to product-style roadmaps
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether the information is organized with a clear hierarchy (TOC/numbered headings), phases/sprints/milestones, and sections mapping directly to her goals (character, plot, pacing, schedule, tools/books) for quick access.

presentation_fit.criterion: Scannability and chunking (TL;DRs, bullets, checklists)
presentation_fit.explanation: Determine if the report offers section TL;DRs, bullet lists, step-by-step checklists, callouts, and short paragraphs to reduce cognitive load and enable quick skimming and reference.

presentation_fit.criterion: Time-aligned layout for weekly schedules and word counts
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether schedules and word-count targets are presented in simple tables or ASCII calendars that reflect her 2-hour weekday evenings and longer weekend blocks, emphasizing presentational clarity over content details.

presentation_fit.criterion: Screenshot-friendly modular cards/blocks
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that guidance is packaged into self-contained blocks with descriptive headings and minimal cross-references, making each block easy to screenshot, save, and share without losing context.

presentation_fit.criterion: Scrivener-native presentation of instructions
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether instructions are framed using Scrivener concepts (Binder tree templates, Corkboard index cards, Labels/Status, Collections) and simple inline examples (e.g., ASCII folder trees) to match her tool mental model.

presentation_fit.criterion: Progress visualization and feedback cues
presentation_fit.explanation: Look for simple visual trackers (ASCII progress bars, milestone checkmarks, burndown-style indicators) that make progress visible and motivating without requiring external tools.

presentation_fit.criterion: Cultural-linguistic localization (Simplified Chinese primary)
presentation_fit.explanation: Confirm that the report uses Simplified Chinese as the main language with consistent punctuation and style, and includes necessary English craft terms with brief Chinese explanations, fitting her daily media context.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Time-feasible roadmap aligned to 10 hours/week and six-month deadline
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the report converts the six months into a realistic phase plan (e.g., outlining, drafting, revision), computes weekly and per-session word goals consistent with 10 hours/week evening blocks, and includes buffer time for weekends/travel so the total word count is attainable.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Clear, measurable milestones and deliverables
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks if the plan defines concrete weekly/monthly outputs (e.g., character bible completed by Week 3, beat sheet by Week 4, 40k words by Week 10) so the user can verify progress and know exactly what to produce by each checkpoint.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Actionable character and relationship development methods
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the report provides step-by-step frameworks (e.g., Goal–Motivation–Conflict, internal/external arc templates, relationship beats, chemistry tests) and worksheets/tasks to deepen the existing prototypes into layered, believable romantic dynamics.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Operational tactics for suspense and pacing control
actionability_practicality.explanation: Determines if the report offers concrete scene-level and chapter-level techniques (e.g., beat sheets for romance, scene–sequel rhythm, hook/reveal cadence, micro-tension checklists, cliffhanger placement) and integrates them into drafting/revision passes.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Scrivener and tool workflow onboarding
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for practical setup steps (project template, folders for acts/scenes, labels for POV/arc, metadata for beats, compile presets, snapshot/backup routine) that reduce friction and directly support the outlined process.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Accessibility, cost, and sequencing of recommended resources
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether books/courses/apps are available in the user’s locale (e.g., Chinese editions/e-books or widely accessible platforms), provide price/trial info, and are sequenced as just-in-time learning tied to plan phases to avoid overload.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Embedded distraction and stress management tactics
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates inclusion of practical session structures (e.g., 25–50 minute sprints, website blockers, micro-rewards), wind-down routines compatible with her 10:30 PM bedtime, and low-pressure alternatives for tough days to maintain consistency.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Feedback and iteration plan
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for a realistic schedule to gather and act on feedback (e.g., beta readers/critique partners, targeted questions, limited sample chapters) leveraging her creative communities, and spells out how feedback feeds into structured revision passes.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Contingency and catch-up strategy
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the plan anticipates disruptions (travel, busy product cycles, illness) with buffer weeks, minimum viable targets, recovery weekends, and re-baselining rules so momentum can be restored without burnout.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Progress tracking and adjustment triggers
actionability_practicality.explanation: Determines if the report provides concrete tracking metrics (weekly words, scenes completed, beats covered) and explicit thresholds to adjust scope/schedule (e.g., if >15% behind by Week 6, recalibrate word targets or compress subplots).

depth_insight.criterion: Quantified, Constraint-Aware Production and Revision Plan
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan rigorously converts six months and ~10 hours/week into realistic drafting and revision milestones (e.g., words/week, buffers, slip recovery), allocates time across outline, drafting, cooling, developmental edits, and line edits, and analyzes trade-offs to hit 80–150k words without sacrificing quality.

depth_insight.criterion: Depth of Character Relationship Design Framework (Romance-Specific)
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the report provides non-generic, high-resolution methods to build compelling romantic dynamics (e.g., wounds/misbeliefs, complementary goals, consent and power dynamics, chemistry mechanisms, relationship conflict matrix, character web) that add psychological depth and drive the romance arc.

depth_insight.criterion: Suspense and Pacing Strategy Tailored to Romance
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the sophistication of techniques to create and sustain tension without drag (micro-tension, scene–sequel ratios, reveal cadence, hook strategies, subplot interplay, slow-burn vs fast-burn calibration, avoiding cheap miscommunication), plus concrete pacing diagnostics and audits.

depth_insight.criterion: Integration of Character Arcs with Plot Beats and External Conflict
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan explicitly maps relationship turning points to plot beats (e.g., meet-cute, midpoint, dark night) with cause–effect chains so stakes escalate from character choices and emotional commitments, producing organic suspense and payoff.

depth_insight.criterion: Iterative Feedback and Validation Mechanisms
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the inclusion of stage-appropriate feedback loops (alpha/beta/sensitivity readers for romance norms and representation, checklists for emotional payoff and consent), measurable checkpoints, and structured revision passes that validate whether suspense and pacing goals are achieved.

depth_insight.criterion: Tool and Workflow Optimization Insight (Scrivener-Centric)
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates depth in configuring Scrivener and complementary tools (metadata for beats/POV, custom templates, snapshots, compile, backups, Plottr/Aeon integration, sprint timers) to concretely improve drafting velocity, version safety, and craft visibility (e.g., color-coding intimacy or tension arcs).

depth_insight.criterion: Resource Curation Quality and Application Guidance
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether recommended books/courses/tools are tightly matched to the writer’s aims (romance relationship arcs, suspense, pacing) and accompanied by application plans (reading sequence, exercises, how to integrate into the weekly schedule).

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization and Feasibility for This Writer
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates tailoring to the writer’s background (short stories), time pattern (weekday evenings/weekends), motivation (daily word target), and stamina, including contingency strategies (micro-goals, sprint days, catch-up buffers) that make the plan realistically executable.

depth_insight.criterion: Originality and High-Value Techniques Beyond Common Advice
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the presence of distinctive, actionable methods (e.g., intimacy ladder, trust/chemistry metrics, conflict heatmap, reveal ledger, pre-mortem for pacing drag) that provide novel leverage rather than repeating standard tips.

logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the report organizes the writing plan into coherent phases (e.g., preparation/outlining, drafting, revision, polishing/beta) with clear objectives, deliverables, milestones, and checkpoints, forming a hierarchy from overall goals to weekly routines.

Evaluates whether each major plan choice (e.g., schedule, daily targets, outlining approach, suspense/pacing techniques, tool usage) is explicitly derived from the user’s stated constraints and goals (six months, 10 hours/week, 80k–150k words, romance focus), making the causal links transparent.

Assesses arithmetic and sequencing coherence: word-count targets vs. available hours (throughput assumptions stated), timeline summation vs. six-month horizon, inclusion of buffers, and logical dependency ordering (e.g., character arcs informing plot beats before drafting).

Checks whether recommended methods and tools (e.g., beat sheets for romance, scene goal–conflict–outcome for pacing, Scrivener features) are supported by concise rationale or references to established craft frameworks that explain why they address the stated problems.

Evaluates the smoothness and logical linkage between sections (goals → plan → methods/tools → resources/monitoring), including cross-references that show how tools and reading recommendations plug into the phases without abrupt jumps.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clean, Hierarchical Formatting and Layout
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report uses a clear heading hierarchy (e.g., phases, weeks, sections), consistent fonts and spacing, and readable paragraph lengths; schedules and checklists are placed where readers expect them. A clean visual structure makes a long, practical plan easy to navigate during daily use.

clarity_readability.criterion: Clarity and Precision of Instructional Language
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether sentences are concise and unambiguous, with actionable phrasing (e.g., 'Draft 1,500 words' vs. 'Write more'), consistent units (hours/week, words/day), and a supportive, professional tone suited to beginners. Clear instructions reduce confusion and enable reliable execution.

clarity_readability.criterion: Correct Use and Explanation of Technical Terms
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether writing-craft terms (e.g., inciting incident, midpoint, scene/sequel, GMC, POV, tropes, black moment, HEA/HFN) are introduced with brief, plain-language definitions or a mini-glossary on first use and then used consistently. Proper term handling lowers the entry barrier for non-experts.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Visual Aids (Tables, Timelines, Trackers, Diagrams)
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks that calendars, word-count tables, timelines, and character-relationship diagrams are legible, correctly labeled (units, dates, legends), not cluttered, and aligned with the text. Good visuals make schedules, pacing, and relationships instantly understandable.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Actions, Milestones, and Checklists
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether critical steps (e.g., weekly goals, draft checkpoints, revision windows) are emphasized via bullets, checkboxes, callouts, or shading so they can be scanned quickly. Visual emphasis helps readers retain and act on the most important tasks.

clarity_readability.criterion: Quality of Summaries and Quick-Start Guidance
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses the presence and clarity of an executive summary or 'at-a-glance' plan, plus section-end recaps that distill the next actions. Strong summaries let readers grasp the whole plan and start immediately without wading through details.

clarity_readability.criterion: Consistency of Labels, References, and Cross-Referencing
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks that section names, week/month labels, and acronyms are used consistently; references (e.g., 'see Week 3 schedule') are accurate and easy to follow. Consistency reduces cognitive friction when switching between the schedule, character work, and tool setup.

task: I hope to complete my first novel within the next six months, with a particular focus on character development and plot construction. The genre I plan to write in is romance, with a target word count of approximately 80,000 to 150,000 words. I already have some initial story ideas, such as the prototypes for the main characters and key plot points, but I want to design reasonable character relationships that add depth to them.I have previously written some short stories and essays but have not yet published any work. In terms of plot construction, I want to solve the problems of how to create suspense and avoid dragging the pacing. I can dedicate around 10 hours per week to writing—about 2 hours each weekday evening, with more concentrated writing on weekends. My preference is to set a daily word-count target (e.g., 1,500 words) to help maintain motivation. As for tools, I lean toward using professional writing software like Scrivener.Please design a writing plan for me, and also recommend relevant books and writing tools.

goal_alignment.criterion: Schedule-persona feasibility and sustainability
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the plan converts '10 hours/week, evenings + weekend focus' into a concrete week-by-week schedule with realistic daily word targets (calibrated to typical WPH), buffers around café evening events and parenting, micro-sessions for café downtimes, and catch-up windows. This respects her work rhythm, time preference, and risk-averse need for buffers, ensuring the plan is actually executable.

goal_alignment.criterion: Romance-specific structure, suspense and pacing solutions
goal_alignment.explanation: Check if the plan applies romance beat frameworks (e.g., Romancing the Beat, Save the Cat Writes a Novel) and pacing tools (scene–sequel, micro-tension, chapter hooks) to explicitly solve 'creating suspense' and 'avoiding drag.' It should provide scene-level checklists and milestone targets appropriate to an 80k–150k romance arc.

goal_alignment.criterion: Character relationship depth tailored to her lived context
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether character/relationship design leverages her single-mother experience, café milieu, pets as motifs, and cross-cultural sensibility, and uses psychological frameworks (GMC, attachment styles, love languages) to build authentic conflicts and emotional arcs. This aligns with her values (emotional connection, psychology) and produces non-generic romance dynamics.

goal_alignment.criterion: Scrivener-centric workflow and health-aware tooling
goal_alignment.explanation: Determine whether the plan provides a Scrivener-first setup (binder templates for beats/POV, labels, compile presets), lightweight integrations (Aeon Timeline/Plottr, mobile capture), backup strategy (iCloud/Dropbox), and ergonomic practices (timed breaks, stretches) to mitigate neck/shoulder strain. This matches her tool preference and health needs while avoiding tool bloat.

goal_alignment.criterion: Motivation and accountability designed for her habits
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate if the plan uses a design-forward, low-friction habit system: calibrated daily targets, visual progress bars, Pomodoro blocks suited to evenings, pre-writing rituals (tea/music like Norah Jones), and gentle social/accountability options (Xiaohongshu/IG progress logs) that fit her aesthetic and privacy comfort. This harnesses her routine and content-sharing behavior.

goal_alignment.criterion: Cultural and sensory setting integration (Shanghai/Hangzhou/café)
goal_alignment.explanation: Check whether the plan actively mines her café, Shanghai old town, and West Lake memories for setting, symbolism, and sensory cues (art, design, coffee culture) and includes methods like location scouting, photo boards, and sensory inventories. This leverages her strongest authentic material and boosts originality and resonance in a romance.

goal_alignment.criterion: Curated, bilingual craft resources and market-aware guidance
goal_alignment.explanation: Assess whether book and tool recommendations are romance- and pacing-relevant (e.g., Romancing the Beat, GMC, The Emotional Craft of Fiction), include Chinese/English options, and offer light market/comp-title research steps aligned with her entrepreneur’s balance of personal vision and audience fit—without overwhelming a first-time novelist.

goal_alignment.criterion: Risk management and contingency planning
goal_alignment.explanation: Verify inclusion of milestone checkpoints, slip-day buffers, scope adjustment rules (e.g., aim for 90–100k if behind), travel/event-aware scheduling, and a phased approach (outline → draft → revision) so a risk-averse user can stay on track despite business demands.

goal_alignment.criterion: Tone, examples, and reasoning style resonance
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether the report’s tone is warm, aesthetically aware, and practical (not corporate), uses examples relevant to cafés/art/urban Shanghai, and presents stepwise logic that matches her rational-yet-creative decision style. This improves acceptance and execution.

content_alignment.criterion: Persona-rooted setting, subgenre, and aesthetic fit
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether the plan and examples deliberately draw on café culture, Shanghai old town/West Lake atmospherics, pets, and art-scene details to propose romance subgenres and motifs (e.g., slow-burn, introspective, detail-rich) that match her Murakami/Bossa Nova sensibility.

content_alignment.criterion: Character relationships grounded in single-mother reality and psychology
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether relationship architectures reflect co-parenting/family dynamics, work-life pressures, and psychological frameworks (attachment, boundaries, love languages) aligned with her lived experience and new interest in psychology.

content_alignment.criterion: Romance-specific suspense and pacing techniques
content_alignment.explanation: Check that suspense/pacing content addresses micro-tensions, near-misses, secrets, subtext, and scene–sequel rhythm suitable for romance, avoiding thriller tropes that misalign with her goals and taste.

content_alignment.criterion: Tool ecosystem alignment centered on Scrivener and visual planning aids
content_alignment.explanation: Determine whether recommendations foreground Scrivener (templates, corkboard, labels) and complementary design-friendly tools (e.g., Plottr/Aeon Timeline/Milanote) and mobile capture that suit her aesthetic and workflow preferences.

content_alignment.criterion: Time/energy-aware content scope and word-target preference fit
content_alignment.explanation: Assess whether content choices respect her 10 hours/week, evening focus, and daily word-target preference—calibrating structures and milestones to her bandwidth rather than generic schedules.

content_alignment.criterion: Bilingual and localized resource curation
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluate whether book/course/community recommendations include both Chinese and English options and, where relevant, local romance-writing communities/platforms, matching her Shanghai base and bilingual background.

content_alignment.criterion: Inspiration pipelines leveraging café, art circles, and travel
content_alignment.explanation: Check that the plan suggests observational prompts and idea-gathering methods tied to her café clientele, art exhibitions, and Melbourne/Europe trips, aligning content with her daily sensory world.

content_alignment.criterion: Ethical and emotional sensibility alignment
content_alignment.explanation: Ensure the content encourages respectful, nuanced portrayals of divorce, parenting, workplace power dynamics, and inclusivity, reflecting her value on emotional connections and open-mindedness.

presentation_fit.criterion: Macro-structure and navigability aligned to a six-month roadmap
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the report uses a clear hierarchy (overview → phases → steps), explicit section headings, numbered sequences, and a phase-based roadmap for the six months so the user can quickly locate planning, character, and plot sections without wading through dense prose.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tone and concision fit for a creative, aesthetics-focused professional
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether the language is warm, encouraging, and precise (short paragraphs, plain sentences), avoiding academic heaviness or condescension while keeping a refined, art-sensitive voice that resonates with the user’s design and cultural background.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology accessibility with brief definitions and optional bilingual glosses
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that craft terms (e.g., inciting incident, beat sheet, POV, GMC, scene–sequel) are introduced with one-line explanations; if English terms are used, include succinct Chinese glosses in parentheses where helpful to reduce cognitive friction.

presentation_fit.criterion: Use of lightweight visual/spatial cues to convey structure
presentation_fit.explanation: Determine whether the report employs text-friendly visuals—bulleted/numbered lists, simple ASCII timelines, indented outlines, and relationship-map layouts—to make pacing arcs and character connections immediately legible for a visually oriented reader.

presentation_fit.criterion: Modular, time-boxed presentation matching evening and weekend sessions
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether tasks are chunked and labeled by typical session lengths (e.g., 2-hour evening blocks, longer weekend blocks) with visible duration tags and micro-goals/checklists so the user can scan and slot modules into her schedule.

presentation_fit.criterion: Tool-oriented formatting that maps to Scrivener’s UI structure
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether instructions are formatted to mirror Scrivener concepts (Binder tree, folders for Acts/Characters/Research, corkboard cards) with clear, numbered steps and consistent naming conventions, easing mental transfer into the software.

presentation_fit.criterion: Mobile-friendly scanability and summary elements
presentation_fit.explanation: Check for short sections, ample whitespace, bullet points, and quick summaries (e.g., TL;DR, key takeaways) so the user can skim on a phone during fragmented time and still capture essential guidance.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Weekly schedule feasibility aligned to 10 hours and evening availability
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether the plan maps the six months into a realistic weekly timetable using 2-hour weekday evenings and longer weekend blocks, includes buffer weeks for café events/childcare, and proposes micro-sessions only when appropriate. Without a workable schedule, execution will fail.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Realistic word-count targets and scope control
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates if daily/weekly word targets are grounded in typical drafting speeds (e.g., 400–800 wph), reconciled with 10 hours/week, and linked to the 80–150k goal. It also checks for a scene budget and scope guards to prevent bloat and enable on-time completion.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Phased plan with milestones and measurable deliverables
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses whether the report defines phases (outline, drafting, revision, beta, polish) with specific dates, outputs (e.g., beat sheet, Act I draft), and acceptance criteria, plus integrates just-in-time learning tasks tied to each phase.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Pacing and suspense implementation toolkit
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for step-by-step methods the user can apply immediately (scene template with goal/conflict/turn, romance beat sheet, mid-point reversal, cliffhanger tactics, pacing audit checklist) to avoid slow sections and create suspense.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Character and relationship development workflow
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan provides concrete exercises (wound/lie, internal vs. external goals, chemistry beats, relationship web, conflict ladder) with timing and deliverables to deepen characters and relationships.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Tool workflow specificity centered on Scrivener
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses if the report gives actionable setup steps (project template, folders by acts/scenes, labels/status, snapshots, compile presets, backups/sync) and low-friction daily routines that reduce technical friction.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Revision and feedback pipeline practicality
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks for a scheduled, time-bounded plan for structural edits, line edits, and copy passes with checklists (continuity, trope delivery, POV consistency), and pragmatic guidance to find/manage beta readers or critique partners and integrate feedback.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Risk management and contingency buffers
actionability_practicality.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan anticipates peak café periods, travel, and childcare constraints; defines minimum viable weekly goals, catch-up strategies, and reserved buffer weeks to maintain momentum under stress.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Accountability and progress tracking mechanisms
actionability_practicality.explanation: Assesses inclusion of simple, executable tracking (word-count log, burndown chart, weekly review checklist), regular check-ins, and rewards to sustain consistency and detect slippage early.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Resource accessibility and acquisition clarity (books/tools)
actionability_practicality.explanation: Checks whether recommended books and tools are easy to obtain in China (or via e-books), note language/edition availability, approximate costs or free trials, and a priority sequence so the user knows what to acquire and when.

depth_insight.criterion: Romance Relationship Architecture and Character Depth Mechanisms
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan goes beyond archetypes to design relationship dynamics and growth: e.g., Goal–Motivation–Conflict mapping for both leads, core wound/misbelief to transformation arcs, consent and power-dynamic considerations, love-arc beats (meet-cute, deepening intimacy, break-up/black moment, grand gesture, HEA/HFN), and tools like relationship matrices or scene-level intimacy/stakes trackers that translate insights into pages.

depth_insight.criterion: Suspense, Tension, and Pacing Methodology for a Romance Narrative
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates concrete mechanisms to create and sustain tension and avoid drag: scene goal–conflict–outcome design, micro-tension at paragraph/line level, promise–payoff tracking, foreshadowing and hooks, act/beat scaffolding (inciting incident, midpoint reversal, pinch points, all-is-lost), sagging-middle countermeasures, and quantifiable pacing diagnostics (e.g., scene length variance, beat density, cliffhanger frequency).

depth_insight.criterion: Feasibility Modeling and Phase Allocation Grounded in Constraints
depth_insight.explanation: Judges whether the plan converts 10 hours/week and the 80–150k goal into realistic throughput: explicit calculations for outlining, drafting, and revision rates; time/word budgets per phase; buffer and contingency for sick weeks/slippage; milestone throughput targets tied to daily/weekly goals; and trade-offs (e.g., lower daily target with longer weekend blocks) justified by productivity assumptions.

depth_insight.criterion: Personalization to Writer’s Experience, Schedule, and Preferences
depth_insight.explanation: Checks that recommendations are tailored to a first-time novelist with short-story background, a 6‑month horizon, evening writing windows, and a preference for daily word targets. Insight is shown by adapting routines (energy management, weekend deep-work blocks), calibrating daily vs weekly targets, addressing common first-novel pitfalls, and proposing mitigation strategies for likely obstacles (burnout, stall at midpoint).

depth_insight.criterion: Milestones, Diagnostic Gates, and Revision Strategy Depth
depth_insight.explanation: Looks for high-value milestone design (e.g., outline complete with beat map and character arcs, draft by week X), objective quality gates (arc integrity checks, tension ledger thresholds, pacing audit), staged revision passes (macro structure, relationship arc, scene-level tension, line/style), and a feedback plan (beta/sensitivity readers aligned to romance content) with clear criteria for incorporating notes.

depth_insight.criterion: Toolchain Insight and Workflow Integration (Scrivener-centric)
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates whether Scrivener and complementary tools are mapped to craft problems: using Binder/Corkboard/custom metadata to track arcs, beats, and POV; snapshots/labels for revision passes; compile strategies; backups; plus integrations (e.g., Plottr/Aeon Timeline for chronology, ProWritingAid for line-level checks) with a defined workflow and data flow rather than a generic list.

depth_insight.criterion: Originality and Actionable Insight Value
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses the presence of distinctive, high-leverage techniques beyond common advice (e.g., tension budget per act, consent checklist for intimacy scenes, wordcount buffer triggers, scene diagnostic scorecards) that would materially improve the chances of finishing on time with a strong romance draft.

logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether the proposed phases, activities, and priorities are explicitly derived from the stated goals (finish a first romance novel, focus on character depth and plot) and constraints (6 months, ~10 hours/week, 80–150k words, daily word-count preference, Scrivener). A logically coherent plan must show clear traceability from inputs to design choices.

Assesses whether word-count targets, weekly hours, and milestones are numerically consistent (e.g., monthly/weekly word-count math, drafting vs revision time allocation, buffers) and free of contradictions (such as daily targets that exceed weekly hour limits). Sound logic requires internally consistent quantitative reasoning.

Checks whether the report orders steps in a dependency-aware sequence (e.g., goal clarification → outline/beat sheet → character web → drafting → pacing/suspense checks → revision), ensuring each phase logically enables the next and avoids circular or premature tasks.

Evaluates whether each recommended book, framework, or tool is logically tied to a specific stated need (e.g., romance tropes, suspense creation, pacing control, Scrivener for project structure) and integrated into the workflow at appropriate phases, rather than appearing as ungrounded suggestions.

logical_coherence.criterion: Metrics, Milestones, and Feedback Loop Logic
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether the plan defines measurable checkpoints (e.g., beat-sheet completion, act deadlines, scene counts), review/adjustment cadences, and decision rules (e.g., when to adjust daily targets or scope) that logically connect progress data to next steps and course corrections.

logical_coherence.criterion: Transition Cohesion and Cross-Referencing Between Sections
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether sections (goals, constraints, plan phases, risk mitigations, tools/books) are connected with explicit transitions and references back to earlier premises, preventing logical jumps and maintaining a continuous reasoning thread through the report.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structural Layout and Navigability
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report uses a clear heading hierarchy, table of contents, section numbering, and consistent section titles (e.g., Schedule, Character Development, Plot/Beats, Pacing/Suspense, Tools/Setup). Includes navigational aids (internal links, cross-references) so users can quickly jump to weekly plans or templates while writing.

clarity_readability.criterion: Language Clarity, Concision, and Actionability
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether instructions are plain, unambiguous, and concise, using measurable and time-bound phrasing (e.g., '1,500 words/day, 5 days/week,' '30-minute sprint x 3'). Looks for imperative verbs, avoidance of hedging, and clarity about deliverables without relying on jargon.

clarity_readability.criterion: Terminology Use and Explanation
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates whether writing-craft and software terms (e.g., GMC, inciting incident, midpoint, HEA/HFN, scene/sequel, POV shifts, Scrivener corkboard/labels/compile) are defined at first use or listed in a glossary, with brief examples to reduce the learning curve.

clarity_readability.criterion: Effectiveness of Visualizations and Planning Artifacts
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether timelines, week-by-week calendars, beat sheets, character relationship diagrams, tables (e.g., weekly targets), and progress trackers are legible, correctly labeled (titles, axes, legends), and aligned with the text so readers can grasp schedules and workflows at a glance.

clarity_readability.criterion: Highlighting of Key Actions, Milestones, and Constraints
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses use of summaries, callouts, bullet lists, and emphasis to surface critical checkpoints (e.g., outline freeze date, midpoint draft deadline, revision windows, tool setup steps), differentiating 'must-do' items from optional tips for quick scanning.

clarity_readability.criterion: Consistency of Units, Notation, and Formatting
clarity_readability.explanation: Evaluates consistent use of units (words/day, hours/week), dates and times, numbering styles, and typographic conventions; and standardized presentation of software menu paths/shortcuts. Consistency reduces cognitive load and prevents misinterpretation.

task: I plan to start a new food and beverage business focusing on healthy fast food within the next six months, with available start-up funds of approximately ¥500,000, including venue rental. My target market is primarily fitness enthusiasts and white-collar workers seeking light, healthy meals. We aim to offer convenient low-calorie healthy meals at a mid-to-high price point, averaging ¥40–¥60 per person.For the menu, I want to focus on salad bowls, energy bowls, and healthy burgers. Our service model will be mainly dine-in plus delivery. For location, I prefer commercial CBD areas and office building clusters to attract a steady flow of customers. I plan to finalize site selection and renovation within three months, aiming to officially open in six months.Please provide a business plan including market research, menu design, marketing strategies, and a detailed financial budget.

goal_alignment.criterion: Constraint-realistic plan for Shanghai CBD within ¥500,000 and 6-month timeline
goal_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether recommendations explicitly reconcile high CBD rents/deposits and build-out costs with the ¥500k cap, and sequence site selection/renovation to meet 3-month and 6-month milestones. Looks for tactics like smaller footprints, subleasing, phased renovations, delivery-first pilots, and realistic deposit multiples and fit-out cost per sqm for Shanghai.

goal_alignment.criterion: Localized market and site analysis tailored to Shanghai CBD/office clusters
goal_alignment.explanation: Checks for Shanghai-specific data (footfall, lunch peak patterns, gym density), competitor benchmarks (e.g., Wagas, Hunter Gatherer), and micro-location comparisons (e.g., Lujiazui, Zhangjiang). Evaluates whether site scoring models, rent-to-sales benchmarks, and traffic composition analyses match white-collar and fitness segments.

goal_alignment.criterion: Menu-persona fit: nutrition transparency, local flavor adaptation, and lunch-rush throughput
goal_alignment.explanation: Verifies that salad/energy bowls and healthy burgers offer calorie/macro labels, protein customization for fitness goals, Jiangnan-compatible flavors, warm options for seasonality, sub-10-minute prep for peak hours, and delivery-friendly formats—all at ¥40–¥60 with portion logic suited to office workers.

goal_alignment.criterion: Channel and partnership strategy leveraging persona strengths and target customer touchpoints
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan uses WeChat Mini Program CRM, Meituan/Ele.me optimization, corporate lunch accounts, and partnerships with gyms/yoga studios/office parks. Looks for marketing experiments, referral loops, and LinkedIn/B2B tactics that align with Liu’s marketing expertise and audience habits.

goal_alignment.criterion: Financial rigor: detailed budget, unit economics, cash flow, break-even, and sensitivity analysis
goal_alignment.explanation: Assesses presence of line-item capex/opex, COGS targets, pricing-to-portion strategy, platform commission impact, break-even volume, and sensitivity to rent, delivery fees, and conversion rates—matching a risk-averse, data-driven MBA’s expectations.

goal_alignment.criterion: Operational design for dine-in + delivery integration and peak-hour reliability
goal_alignment.explanation: Checks SOPs, staffing/shifts, prep workflows, mise en place, queue design, seating turnover, and packaging standards to maintain speed and quality. Ensures the operating model supports lunch peak demand in CBD and high delivery SLAs.

goal_alignment.criterion: Execution roadmap aligned to Liu’s time management and delegation needs
goal_alignment.explanation: Looks for a milestone-based Gantt, vendor/contractor shortlists, critical path, and delegable task structure that respects his morning focus, family evenings, and managerial workload. Includes decision gates and weekly KPI check-ins to keep on schedule.

goal_alignment.criterion: Risk controls, pilots, and stage-gate decision framework
goal_alignment.explanation: Evaluates the inclusion of a delivery-first soft launch or pop-up, landlord negotiation levers, lease clause safeguards, contingency budgets, and clear go/no-go metrics—matching his risk-averse, long-term orientation.

goal_alignment.criterion: Regulatory and compliance specificity for Shanghai F&B
goal_alignment.explanation: Checks for accurate steps and timelines for Shanghai food business licensing, hygiene permits, staff health cards, HACCP practices, and delivery food safety requirements to avoid opening delays.

goal_alignment.criterion: Brand and experience resonance with East-West health narrative and sustainability
goal_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether the brand story blends Jiangnan sensibilities with Western nutrition concepts, and whether packaging aesthetics and sustainability align with white-collar expectations and Liu’s quality values.

goal_alignment.criterion: Structured, case-based communication with frameworks and KPIs
goal_alignment.explanation: Checks for use of STP/7P/AARRR, competitor case studies, dashboards, and hypothesis-driven testing language, matching Liu’s MBA background and preference for in-depth, analytical presentation.

content_alignment.criterion: Financial Model Tailored to ¥500k Budget, Shanghai CBD Costs, and ¥40–¥60 Ticket
content_alignment.explanation: Checks whether the plan provides Shanghai-CBD-relevant CAPEX/OPEX, platform commission and packaging cost assumptions, per-SKU unit economics, target gross margin ranges, breakeven point, runway, and sensitivity scenarios that match a risk-averse MBA user’s need for rigor and budget fit.

content_alignment.criterion: Shanghai CBD-Specific Demand and Competitor Mapping for Fitness and White-Collar Lunch/Dinner Dayparts
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether market research uses local office density, lunch peak flows, gym/yoga adjacency, and the ¥40–¥60 price band to size TAM/SAM/SOM and benchmark relevant healthy-fast brands near CBD clusters, ensuring precise local relevance rather than generic national data.

content_alignment.criterion: Menu Architecture Tuned to Fitness/White-Collar Needs and Jiangnan Palate with Nutritional Precision
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether the menu (salad bowls, energy bowls, healthy burgers) includes macro/calorie targets, protein-forward SKUs, warm options for seasonality, Jiangnan-inspired flavors, and delivery-suitable items at the ¥40–¥60 range, matching both target segments and the user’s taste background.

content_alignment.criterion: Channel and Partnership Strategy Aligned to WeChat/LinkedIn Use and CBD Ecosystem
content_alignment.explanation: Verifies that marketing content prioritizes WeChat private domain operations, enterprise meal plans/group ordering, gym/yoga studio collaborations, and LinkedIn corporate outreach with clear targeting logic for CBD white-collar and fitness cohorts, reflecting the user’s platforms and networks.

content_alignment.criterion: Dine-in + Delivery Operational Content for CBD Lunch Peaks and Delivery SLAs
content_alignment.explanation: Checks for throughput modeling (orders per minute, line design for bowls/burgers), order-ahead/pickup flows, delivery radius/SLA, packaging cost and temperature retention—content specifically chosen for a CBD office lunch rush and dual service model.

content_alignment.criterion: Evidence-Backed Case Studies and Benchmarks Relevant to Shanghai/Tier-1 Healthy Fast-Food
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses inclusion of data-driven case examples (e.g., unit economics, customer acquisition costs, retention metrics) from comparable Shanghai or Tier-1 city operators, matching the user’s preference for in-depth analysis and case-study learning.

content_alignment.criterion: Staged Validation and De-risking Content Matching Risk Appetite and 6-Month Window
content_alignment.explanation: Evaluates whether the plan includes MVPs (office-tower pop-ups, gym co-branded trials, pre-sale meal cards), test metrics and kill/scale thresholds to systematically reduce risk before full-scale launch, aligning with his risk-averse, data-led approach.

content_alignment.criterion: Shanghai-Specific Compliance, Food Safety, and Nutrition Claim Guidance
content_alignment.explanation: Checks for content on required local permits, food safety protocols, labeling and nutrition claim standards applicable in Shanghai, reflecting the user’s pragmatic risk control needs.

content_alignment.criterion: Brand Positioning Reflecting East–West Fusion and Professional White-Collar Identity
content_alignment.explanation: Assesses whether brand narrative and value propositions link Jiangnan lightness and craftsmanship with Western healthy eating concepts in a way that resonates with CBD professionals and the user’s cultural perspective.

presentation_fit.criterion: Executive summary and top-down storyline (SCQA) clarity
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether the report opens with a concise TL;DR (key metrics, positioning, target users, price band, site type, launch timeline) and follows a clear Situation–Complication–Question–Answer flow. An MBA marketing manager expects a crisp, top-down narrative to grasp the whole plan quickly and decide where to dive deeper.

presentation_fit.criterion: Professional, concise business tone (no hype, evidence-led phrasing)
presentation_fit.explanation: Check if the language is precise, neutral, and analytical, avoiding buzzwords and exaggerated claims, while highlighting data-backed statements. This aligns with the user’s preference for in-depth, case-style materials and enhances credibility.

presentation_fit.criterion: Terminology calibration and first-use definitions
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether marketing/finance/F&B operations terms (e.g., CAC, LTV, contribution margin, COGS, SKU, table turnover, SOP) are used correctly and briefly defined/formula-noted on first use. This maintains rigor without forcing extra research, fitting an MBA-level but cross-domain reader.

presentation_fit.criterion: Information architecture and navigation
presentation_fit.explanation: Review the presence of a logical business-plan structure (e.g., Executive Summary → Market → Positioning → Menu/Operations → Location → Marketing → Financials → Risks → Timeline → Appendices), with numbered headings, a table of contents, consistent section labels, and cross-references. This supports efficient skimming and deep dives during fragmented reading time.

presentation_fit.criterion: Data visualization quality for market and operations
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess clarity and readability of charts/tables (axes, units, labels, legends, annotations, consistent colors, minimal chart junk, colorblind-friendly palettes). Include callouts that summarize the takeaway near each figure. This matches the user’s habit of screenshotting and sharing insights.

presentation_fit.criterion: Financials presentation clarity (assumptions vs outputs, scenarios)
presentation_fit.explanation: Check that financials are presented with clean unit-economics tables, clearly separated assumptions vs results, scenario/sensitivity displays, and a break-even chart, all with RMB units and per-cover metrics. Formatting should let the reader trace numbers quickly and screenshot key tables.

presentation_fit.criterion: Timeline and milestone visualization (Gantt/readable roadmap)
presentation_fit.explanation: Evaluate whether the six-month plan is shown as a clear Gantt or roadmap with monthly milestones, dependencies, and critical path markers. The visual should be legible at a glance and support planning conversations.

presentation_fit.criterion: Localization of format and language for Mainland China context
presentation_fit.explanation: Check for Mainland formatting conventions (¥ currency, Chinese units like m², 24-hour time, local platform names such as Meituan/Ele.me) and Mandarin or bilingual terminology where appropriate. This reduces cognitive friction and fits the user’s Shanghai operating environment.

presentation_fit.criterion: Reusable checklists, scorecards, and templates formatting
presentation_fit.explanation: Assess whether site-scoring matrices, supplier checklists, SOP templates, and marketing calendars are presented as clean, fillable, and standalone appendices. Formatting should enable easy printing/screenshotting and immediate adoption, matching the user’s efficient, share-oriented workflow.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Financial feasibility within ¥500,000 with unit economics and cash runway
actionability_practicality.explanation: The plan must itemize CAPEX/OPEX (rent/deposit, fit-out, equipment, licenses, initial inventory, packaging, marketing, working capital) and show 12-month P&L, cash flow, gross margin, labor %, rent %, delivery commissions, and a break-even analysis with 2–3 traffic scenarios to ensure the opening and first months are fundable without a cash shortfall.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Site selection and lease execution framework for Shanghai CBD/office clusters
actionability_practicality.explanation: Provides a data-backed screening scorecard (footfall at lunch, office density, gym proximity, rent per m², competitor density, delivery radius/ETA, power/ventilation feasibility) and a lease checklist (deposit, rent-free period, termination/assignment clauses) with target thresholds and a step-by-step process to sign a compliant site within three months.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Menu-engineering with costed recipes meeting price point and nutrition goals
actionability_practicality.explanation: Includes BOM and target food cost % per SKU, prep/assembly time standards, caloric/macronutrient info for fitness/white-collar needs, and delivery suitability, ensuring salad/energy bowls and healthy burgers can be produced quickly with consistent quality and margins at ¥40–¥60.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Operations and staffing plan for peak lunch throughput and limited owner time
actionability_practicality.explanation: Defines headcount by role and shift, SOPs for prep/line service/QA/cleaning, training plan, and labor budget, enabling reliable operations during weekday peaks while allowing the owner to manage via a store manager and standardized procedures.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Delivery model economics and execution
actionability_practicality.explanation: Specifies aggregator mix and commission assumptions, pricing and menu adaptation for delivery, packaging specs and cost per order, delivery radius/SLAs, and operational steps to integrate online orders, ensuring delivery contributes profitably rather than eroding margins.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Compliance and permitting roadmap tailored to Shanghai
actionability_practicality.explanation: Outlines required licenses and inspections (business license, food business permit, health certificates), fire/ventilation/grease-trap requirements given the menu (e.g., burger griddle), landlord approvals, costs, sequencing with renovation, and realistic lead times to de-risk schedule.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Supply chain setup and equipment plan
actionability_practicality.explanation: Provides supplier shortlists and backup options for produce/proteins/packaging, delivery cadence, cold-chain handling, waste controls, plus an equipment list with quotes, power loads, and maintenance plan, ensuring opening readiness and operational continuity.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Go-to-market plan with channel budgets, KPIs, and 90-day calendar
actionability_practicality.explanation: Translates marketing into executable actions for CBD office workers and fitness communities (e.g., gym partnerships, corporate lunch deals, WeChat mini-program, aggregator ads), with budget allocations, CAC/LTV targets, and a week-by-week launch calendar to drive trial and repeat.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Project timeline and critical path to open in six months
actionability_practicality.explanation: Includes a Gantt-like schedule with dependencies (site -> design -> permits -> fit-out -> inspections -> training -> soft opening), decision gates, and buffer time, plus owner/assignee for each task to ensure site and renovation finish within three months and opening by month six.

actionability_practicality.criterion: Risk management and sensitivity analysis with contingency actions
actionability_practicality.explanation: Models upside/base/downside for sales, food cost inflation, delivery take rates, and delays; defines triggers and countermeasures (menu simplification, hours adjustment, rent re-negotiation, promo levers) and sets a contingency reserve, aligning with a risk-averse execution style.

actionability_practicality.criterion: KPI instrumentation and management cadence
actionability_practicality.explanation: Defines a minimal dashboard (daily sales, AOV, gross margin, labor %, food cost %, waste, delivery ratings, repeat rate) with data sources, reporting frequency, and actions at thresholds, enabling the user to manage remotely and make rapid, data-backed adjustments.

depth_insight.criterion: CBD demand sizing and micro-segmentation with temporal granularity
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the report quantifies addressable demand (catchment, lunch vs dinner peaks, weekday vs weekend, seasonality), distinguishes fitness vs white-collar needs and willingness to pay at ¥40–¥60, and leverages credible proxies (footfall, nearby gyms/offices, platform order density).

depth_insight.criterion: Menu engineering for nutrition, margin, and throughput
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates if salad/energy bowls and healthy burgers are designed around macro/calorie targets, per-SKU contribution margin, ingredient commonality, prep workflow, equipment, and delivery integrity; identifies hero SKUs, add-ons, and SKU rationalization to balance variety with complexity.

depth_insight.criterion: Channel-specific unit economics and pricing strategy
depth_insight.explanation: Judges depth in modeling dine-in vs delivery economics (platform commissions, packaging, discounts), channel price/menu differentiation, AOV and attach-rate tactics, and the feasibility of margins at the stated price point.

depth_insight.criterion: Financial model depth with break-even and sensitivity/scenario analysis
depth_insight.explanation: Checks for a rigorous CAPEX/OPEX build (rent, fit-out, equipment, labor, COGS), break-even orders and occupancy cost ratio, cash runway vs ¥500k, and sensitivity to volume, COGS, rent, and commission; includes base/conservative/optimistic scenarios.

depth_insight.criterion: Location selection insight and micro-site analytics
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether location analysis goes beyond ‘CBD preference’ to quantify rent-to-sales targets, visibility/frontage, ingress/egress, rider parking/elevator bottlenecks, delivery radius, utilities/ventilation, and competitor proximity to justify site viability.

depth_insight.criterion: Supply chain robustness and waste/shrinkage management
depth_insight.explanation: Evaluates sourcing for fresh produce and proteins (vendor redundancy, cold chain), forecast and prep strategies to control waste, food safety controls, and trade-offs between pre-processing and in-house that affect cost, quality, and throughput.

depth_insight.criterion: Marketing strategy originality and ROI orientation
depth_insight.explanation: Looks for segment-specific tactics (fitness and corporate), partnerships (gyms, office admins), platform operations (SEM, coupons), community/UGC, membership/CRM, and quantification of CAC, LTV, retention, and new-store launch ramp with measurable targets.

depth_insight.criterion: Execution feasibility under timeline and capital constraints
depth_insight.explanation: Assesses whether the plan identifies critical-path tasks (licenses, build-out, equipment lead times, hiring/training), budgets buffers, and aligns the six-month opening and three-month site/renovation goals with realistic dependencies.

depth_insight.criterion: Risk identification and mitigation with triggers
depth_insight.explanation: Judges whether key risks (remote work reducing lunch traffic, commission changes, weather seasonality, competitor price wars, supply disruptions) are quantified where possible and paired with concrete mitigations and leading indicators.

depth_insight.criterion: Explicit assumptions and test-and-learn plan
depth_insight.explanation: Checks if critical assumptions (daily orders, AOV, COGS%, conversion, retention) are stated and linked to a pre/post-launch experimentation plan to validate and iterate quickly for product, pricing, and operations.

logical_coherence.criterion: End-to-End Business Plan Framework and Ordering
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks whether the report follows a coherent sequence (market research → positioning → offering/menu & pricing → location & channels → operations → marketing plan → financials → risks → timeline) so that each section sets up the next. A clear structure is the backbone that prevents logical fragmentation.

logical_coherence.criterion: Traceability from Market Research to Strategic Choices
logical_coherence.explanation: Assesses whether menu focus (salad/energy bowls, healthy burgers), price band (¥40–¥60), location (CBD/office clusters), and channel mix (dine-in + delivery) are explicitly derived from target customer insights and market analysis, not asserted independently. Decisions must be justified by prior premises.

Evaluates whether key claims (demand, CBD rents, competitor prices, delivery commissions, foot traffic, conversion rates) are supported by relevant evidence or clearly justified assumptions, and whether the report explicitly links each claim to its supporting data. This guards against unsupported leaps.

logical_coherence.criterion: Financial Logic Coherence and Numeric Consistency
logical_coherence.explanation: Tests whether financials are derived stepwise from stated assumptions (e.g., revenue = average ticket × transactions; COGS → gross margin; fixed costs → break-even; capex/opex split; deposits/renovation within ¥500k), that numbers reconcile across sections, and that P&L, cash flow, and budget timing are internally consistent.

logical_coherence.criterion: Operational Feasibility Chain (Capacity, Staffing, Service Model, Location)
logical_coherence.explanation: Checks the logic linking forecast demand in CBD offices to seat count, kitchen throughput, prep times, delivery radius/SLAs, and staffing schedules. The report should show that expected volume is operationally achievable with the proposed resources and service model.

Assesses whether transitions explicitly reference prior conclusions/assumptions (e.g., pricing informing unit economics; delivery mix informing staffing) and preview how the next section builds on them, preventing abrupt topic shifts and logical gaps.

logical_coherence.criterion: Timeline and Dependency Logic Aligned with 6-Month Launch
logical_coherence.explanation: Evaluates whether milestones (site selection, lease, design, renovation, licensing, equipment, hiring/training, soft opening) have realistic durations and dependencies, with overlapping tasks only where feasible and cash outflows timed to the schedule, ensuring the 6‑month target is logically defensible.

clarity_readability.criterion: Structural Layout and Navigability
clarity_readability.explanation: Checks whether the report has a clean, consistent hierarchy (TOC, numbered headings, subheadings) for key sections (Executive Summary, Market Research, Menu Design, Operations/Service Model, Marketing Strategy, Financial Budget, Timeline), with clear page numbers, consistent headers/footers, adequate white space, and bulleting—so readers can quickly locate items like budget tables or the six‑month Gantt.

clarity_readability.criterion: Language Clarity, Conciseness, and Tone
clarity_readability.explanation: Assesses whether sentences are straightforward and free of ambiguity, grammar/spelling errors are minimized, and terminology is used consistently (e.g., using one term for AOV/客单价). Ensures concise, action‑oriented phrasing suitable for operators and investors, improving comprehension without domain expertise.
